bills.title: ELON MUSK Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill is about special government employees who work for the government part time or for short periods. These workers can only work for the government less than 130 days per year. The bill says these special employees cannot have contracts to do business with the federal government. If they already have contracts with the government, those contracts must end. This stops part-time government workers from also being paid contractors for the same government.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.title: Of inquiry requesting the President and directing the Secretaries of the Treasury and Labor to transmit, respectively, certain documents to the House of Representatives relating to the effect on local economies and communities of the Department of Government Efficiency and newly-imposed tariffs.
bills.plain_summary: Congress wants the President and two government departments to hand over papers that show how recent changes are hurting local towns and cities. They want to know about people losing their jobs because the government is firing workers and cutting programs. They also want to learn about how new taxes on goods from other countries are making things more expensive for families. The House of Representatives is demanding this information so they can understand what these new policies are doing to communities across America.

bills.title: SEC Modernization Act
bills.plain_summary: The SEC Modernization Act requires the Securities and Exchange Commission to reorganize how its offices are set up. The bill makes the Commission move some offices to work under different departments and combine some offices together. The Commission can make more changes to its organization later if it wants to. The bill also lets the Commission combine its regional offices that are located in different parts of the country.

bills.plain_summary: This resolution shows that the House of Representatives agrees with President Trump's actions to fight terrorism in early 2025. The President labeled certain dangerous criminal groups as terrorist organizations and worked to remove their members from the United States. The House supports using a law called the Alien Enemies Act to arrest and send away foreign terrorists. Some members of a group called Tren de Aragua were sent to El Salvador to be put in prison. bills.title: Protecting Critical Infrastructure Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the punishments for hacking into important American systems much more severe. If someone uses a computer to attack things like power grids or water systems, they could go to prison for at least thirty years or even for life. The bill also says the President must punish people from other countries who hack into these important systems by freezing their money and not letting them come to America. The goal is to better protect the critical infrastructure that Americans depend on every day.

bills.title: Supporting the designation of May 16, 2025, as "Endangered Species Day".
bills.plain_summary: This bill supports making May 16, 2025 a special day called Endangered Species Day. The day would celebrate how well the Endangered Species Act has worked to save plants and animals from dying out completely. The bill points out that more than two thousand species are still in danger. However, it also shows that conservation efforts have been very successful, saving ninety-nine percent of protected species from extinction.

bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop government agencies from using estimates about how much greenhouse gases like carbon dioxide cost society when they make new rules or decisions. It requires agencies to tell Congress how many times they have used these cost estimates since 2009. The bill also tells agencies they must follow certain government guidelines when they study how their actions might affect the environment. The goal is to change how the government considers the costs of pollution when making rules that affect businesses and the public.

bills.plain_summary: This congressional resolution says that the United States should pay reparations to Black Americans whose families were enslaved. The resolution explains how slavery and discrimination have hurt Black people for hundreds of years and continue to cause problems today. It says the government has a duty to make up for these wrongs by giving money and other help to people whose ancestors were slaves. bills.title: Ride-Along Resolution
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make every member of the House of Representatives spend one work day riding in a police car with a police officer in their home area. They would have to do this once every two years while they are in office. If a House member does not complete this police ride-along, their name would be put on a public list that everyone can see. bills.title: Protecting America’s Cybersecurity Act
bills.plain_summary: The Protecting America's Cybersecurity Act would help people who got fired from CISA, a government agency that protects computers and the internet from hackers. If someone was fired from CISA between late January and early March 2025, this bill would let them get their job back and receive the pay they missed. The bill also says that in the future, no one can be fired or moved from CISA unless Congress agrees to it first. Additionally, people who work for the Department of Government Efficiency would not be allowed to work at CISA.

bills.short_title: Overtime Pay Tax Relief Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives workers a tax break on overtime pay they earn at their jobs. Workers can deduct some of their overtime pay from their taxes, but only up to 20% of their regular wages from the same employer. The tax break is only available to people who make less than $100,000 per year, or $150,000 for single parents, or $200,000 for married couples filing together. This tax deduction will only last until the end of 2029.

bills.title: BOOST Act
bills.plain_summary: The BOOST Act creates a tax credit to help people in rural areas get better internet service at home. If you live in an area with slow internet speeds below 25 megabits per second, you can get money back on your taxes for buying equipment that boosts internet signals. The government will pay back 75 percent of what you spend on things like Wi-Fi boosters or satellite equipment, up to 400 dollars total. This tax credit will be available through 2029 and is designed to help people in areas where internet service is poor or unavailable.

This means companies can continue to use the best security methods to protect people's personal data from hackers and other threats.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for Medicare patients starting in January 2026. Right now, the government can say that certain medical services must be done only when patients stay overnight in the hospital. bills.title: Congressional Oversight of the Antiquities Act

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for when the President creates new national monuments. Currently, when a President designates land as a national monument, it stays that way permanently. Under this new bill, any national monument created by the President would only last for six months or until the current Congress ends, whichever comes first. If Congress wants to keep the monument, they must pass a law to make it permanent. If Congress does not act and the monument expires, that same land cannot be made into a national monument again for twenty five years.

bills.title: St. Patrick’s Day Act
bills.plain_summary: The St. Patrick's Day Act would make St. Patrick's Day a new federal holiday in the United States. This means that federal government workers would get the day off from work, just like they do for holidays such as Christmas and Thanksgiving. The new holiday would be added to the official list of federal holidays right after Washington's Birthday. If this bill becomes law, St. Patrick's Day would join the other days when federal offices and many government services are closed.

bills.title: Disaster Assistance Fairness Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps people who live in condos, townhomes, and other shared housing get government help after big disasters. When storms or other disasters damage these buildings, the government can now help pay to remove debris and fix shared parts like roofs, elevators, and heating systems. Before this bill, it was harder for people in these types of homes to get the same disaster help that people in regular houses could get. Now the rules are more fair for everyone.

bills.plain_summary: This resolution honors the eightieth anniversary of an important World War Two battle that happened on a Japanese island called Iwo Jima in 1945. The Senate wants to celebrate the friendship between the United States and Japan today, and it encourages Americans to honor these veterans with special ceremonies and activities.

bills.title: Stop Illegal Campaign Coordination Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules about how outside groups can work with political candidates during elections. Right now, these groups are supposed to work independently from candidates, but sometimes they secretly work together, which breaks the law. The bill creates clearer rules to catch when outside groups are illegally coordinating with candidates by following their private instructions about ads, messaging, or who to target with their spending. bills.title: Medicare Fraud Detection and Deterrence Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Medicare Fraud Detection and Deterrence Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules to catch Medicare fraud and stop bad actors from cheating the system. When doctors or other healthcare providers get banned from Medicare for fraud or abuse, the government must shut down their special ID numbers so they cannot keep billing Medicare. The bill also requires Medicare Advantage plans to include provider ID numbers when they report certain services like medical equipment, lab tests, and home health care. Finally, doctors who work for telehealth companies must use special billing codes when they provide care over the phone or computer so the government can better track these services.

bills.title: Saving DOE’s Workforce Act
bills.plain_summary: The Saving DOE's Workforce Act stops the Department of Energy from laying off workers or firing them until Congress creates a complete budget for 2026. The only time the Department of Energy can still fire someone is if that person does something wrong at work or does a bad job. This law protects most Department of Energy employees from losing their jobs while Congress works on the budget. The rule will stay in place until the government has a full spending plan approved for fiscal year 2026.

bills.title: Community First Act
bills.plain_summary: The Community First Act creates a new program where the Attorney General gives money to local groups to help reduce the number of people in jail. The program gives two kinds of grants: smaller planning grants up to $100,000 to study jail numbers and make plans, and bigger grants from $500,000 to $3 million over six years to actually carry out those plans. Local groups can use the money for things like reducing bail money requirements and creating programs that keep people out of jail. To keep getting the grant money, groups must reduce their jail population by at least 5% in the first year, 10% each year after that, and 50% total by the end.

bills.short_title: Tax Relief from Tariffs and High Costs Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a special tax credit for 2025 that helps people save money on their taxes. If you qualify, you can get back 10 percent of what you owe in federal income taxes. To get this credit, you must earn less than $100,000 if you are single or less than $200,000 if you are married. The bill is meant to help people deal with high costs and expensive tariffs.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Audit the IRS Act
bills.plain_summary: The Audit the IRS Act is a new law about who can work for the IRS. It says that people who owe a lot of overdue taxes cannot get jobs at the IRS or keep working there. The person in charge of the IRS must check regularly to make sure all workers and people who want jobs there have paid their taxes. This rule helps make sure that IRS employees follow the same tax rules they expect other people to follow.

bills.title: Secure Storage Information Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This new law makes gun stores do three important things to help keep guns safe. First, gun stores must give information about safe gun storage to everyone who buys a gun. Second, they must sell different types of gun safes and locks in their stores. Third, people who buy gun safes can get up to five hundred dollars back on their taxes to help pay for them.

bills.title: A resolution expressing support for the designation of the month of April 2025 as "Parkinson's Awareness Month".
bills.plain_summary: This resolution asks Congress to make April 2025 "Parkinson's Awareness Month." Parkinson's disease is a serious illness that affects more than one million Americans and causes problems with movement and other symptoms. The disease is becoming more common and also affects millions of family members who care for people with Parkinson's. The Senate wants to support research to find better treatments and a cure for this disease. They also want to recognize all the people who work to help those living with Parkinson's disease.

bills.title: No Penalties for Victims of Fraud Act
bills.short_title: No Penalties for Victims of Fraud Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps people who have been victims of fraud or scams. If someone needs to take money out of their retirement account early because they were cheated, they will not have to pay the usual penalty fees. To get this help, victims must show proof from police or courts that they were really victims of fraud. The Treasury Department will make rules about how this works and teach people about these new protections.

bills.title: Death Tax Repeal Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill gets rid of two taxes that rich people have to pay when they die or give money to their grandchildren or great-grandchildren. Right now, families have to pay these taxes when someone dies and leaves behind a lot of money or property. The bill also changes the rules so people can give away up to ten million dollars while they are still alive without paying any gift tax. This means wealthy families would be able to keep more of their money when passing it down to their children and grandchildren.

bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the Securities and Exchange Commission from making companies share information about climate change and the environment. Companies would only have to report climate information if it directly affects whether people should buy or sell their stock. The bill is meant to limit what environmental details companies must tell investors. It is called the Stop Environmental Calculations Act of 2025.

bills.plain_summary: This law tells the EPA to create a program where clothing companies can choose to put special labels on their clothes. These labels will show customers how much pollution the clothes make during their whole life, from when they are made until they are thrown away. Companies must tell the government before they change how their devices are designed or how to clean them. bills.plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules to help families in Puerto Rico get the same child tax credit as families in other parts of America. Right now, Puerto Rico families cannot get the full amount of money back from this tax credit that helps parents pay for their children's needs. Starting in 2025, Puerto Rico families will be able to get the same full credit as everyone else in the United States. bills.plain_summary: The Health Share Transparency Act of 2025 makes new rules for health care sharing ministries. These groups help people share medical costs but they are not regular insurance companies. The new law says these ministries must report their money details and how they operate to the government. They also have to tell their members clearly that the ministry might not pay for their medical bills and explain how to complain if there are problems. The government will also share reports about complaints against these ministries twice each year so people can see if there are issues.

bills.plain_summary: This law would close down the Department of Education within one year. All the programs that the Department of Education currently runs would be moved to other government departments instead. Schools would get money through block grants that states control, while student loans would be handled by the Treasury Department. bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to make April 2025 a special month to teach people about esophageal cancer. This program looks for viruses like COVID-19, flu, mpox, dengue, West Nile virus, and RSV in wastewater to help track diseases in communities. The CDC works with local health departments to collect water samples and share information about what germs they find. The program gets $150 million each year from 2026 to 2030 to do this work, but water companies do not have to join if they do not want to.

bills.title: EITC Modernization Act
bills.plain_summary: The EITC Modernization Act makes it easier for more people to get help with their taxes through the earned income tax credit. This tax credit gives money back to people who do not make a lot of money from working. The bill lets college students and people who take care of family members besides children get this help too. It also gives money to groups that help low income people prepare their taxes for free.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: EASE Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The EASE Act of 2025 is a new law that changes how hospitals help certain Medicare patients. When Medicare patients are leaving the hospital, the hospital must tell them about hospice care programs if they might qualify for this type of care. Hospice care is special medical care for people who are very sick and helps them feel more comfortable. This new rule will start on January 1, 2026.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_keywords_str: hospital discharge, Medicare patients, hospice care, end of life care, comfort care, terminal illness, hospital requirements, patient information, dying patients, palliative care

bills.title: Remote Opioid Monitoring Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells a government office called the Government Accountability Office to study technology that can watch people from far away when they take strong pain medicines called opioids. The study needs to find out how well this watching technology works and how many people use it. The office also needs to give ideas about how to make this technology easier for people to get through government health programs. The goal is to help keep people safe when they take these powerful pain medicines.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_keywords_str: opioid monitoring, pain medication tracking, remote patient monitoring, prescription drug surveillance, opioid safety technology, medication compliance tracking, pain medicine oversight, digital health monitoring, opioid abuse prevention, prescription monitoring programs, patient safety technology, drug monitoring devices

bills.title: Old Drugs, New Cures Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a special group called priority research drugs for old medicines that drug companies want to use to treat different diseases than they were first made for. When companies test these old drugs for new uses, the government will give them special benefits in healthcare programs like Medicare and Medicaid. These drugs will not have to follow the same strict rules about paying money back to the government that other changed drugs have to follow. This is meant to encourage companies to find new ways to use medicines that already exist instead of always making completely new ones.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such

bills.title: Improving Access to Addiction Medicine Providers Act
bills.short_title: Improving Access to Addiction Medicine Providers Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes a program that gives money to help minority students become doctors and other healthcare workers. The program will now also help train students to work in addiction medicine, which means helping people with drug and alcohol problems. It will also include training for mental health work where students learn to figure out what mental health problems people have. This will help create more healthcare workers who can help people with addiction and mental health issues.

bills.title: Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Strengthening VA Patient Advocacy for Rural Veterans Act of 2025 helps veterans who live in rural areas get better support when they need medical care. The bill requires VA hospitals to have special people called coordinators who will help rural veterans when they receive care away from the main hospital. It also makes sure patient advocates answer directly to the hospital directors so they can do their jobs better. Finally, the VA must write reports every year to show how well they are helping veterans with their medical problems and concerns.

bills.title: Racehorse Tax Parity Act
bills.plain_summary: The Racehorse Tax Parity Act changes tax rules to help people who own racehorses. Right now, owners must keep their horses for twenty four months to get better tax treatment, but this bill would cut that time in half to just twelve months. This means racehorse owners could get tax benefits much sooner than before. The new rules would start working for taxes filed after December 31, 2024.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Abortion Is Not Health Care Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Abortion Is Not Health Care Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules about abortions. Right now, people can count abortion costs as medical expenses when they file their taxes. Under this new bill, abortion costs would not count as medical expenses for tax purposes. However, there are two exceptions where abortion costs could still be counted. These exceptions are when the pregnancy happened because of rape or incest, or when the mother's life is in danger.

bills.title: A joint resolution directing the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities in Ukraine that have not been authorized by Congress.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution tells the President that US military forces must stop fighting in Ukraine within 30 days. The only way US troops can stay and fight is if Congress officially declares war or passes a special law saying it's okay. Right now, Congress has not given permission for US forces to be in combat in Ukraine. The resolution does not allow any new military actions to happen. It is meant to make sure Congress has a say in whether American troops fight in other countries.

bills.title: DOGE Act
bills.plain_summary: The DOGE Act is a new law that takes an executive order made by the president and turns it into an official law passed by Congress. Executive Order 14210 was something the president ordered, but now Congress is making it a permanent law with the same power as other laws they pass. This means the rules in that executive order will now be official law that is harder to change or remove. The bill is called the DOGE Act as its short name.

bills.plain_summary: This bill is about removing a federal judge named Paul Adam Engelmayer from his job. bills.plain_summary: This bill is a way for Congress to honor and remember Hank Aaron, one of the greatest baseball players ever. In 1974, Aaron broke Babe Ruth's home run record, but he faced a lot of racism during his career. bills.plain_summary: This bill extends a government program that helps clean up dirty land called brownfields through 2030. It makes it easier for small towns and disadvantaged communities to get money by lowering how much they have to pay and removing cost sharing requirements completely for these areas. bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the IRS check the tax returns of the President and people close to them every year. The IRS must then share a report about what they found and also make the actual tax returns public so everyone can see them. bills.title: Dignity in Housing Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Dignity in Housing Act of 2025 is a new law about public housing where people with low incomes live. The law says that big public housing buildings with 100 or more homes must be checked by inspectors who don't work for the housing authority at least every two years. These inspectors will look at the buildings to make sure they are safe and in good condition. After the inspections are done, the results must be put online so anyone can read them. This will help make sure public housing is kept in better shape for the people who live there.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for farmers who want to get help from government disaster programs. Right now, farmers who make more than $900,000 per year cannot get this disaster money. The new law says that farmers can still get disaster payments even if they make more than $900,000, as long as at least 75% of their money comes from farming, ranching, or forestry work. This applies to five different disaster programs that help farmers when bad weather or other problems hurt their crops or animals.

bills.title: Extending Limits of United States Customs Waters Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Extending Limits of United States Customs Waters Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill doubles the area where U.S. border patrol can chase and stop boats from twelve miles to twenty-four miles off the American coast. Congress wants to make this change because today's boats are much faster than before and can easily escape from law enforcement in the current twelve-mile zone. The new twenty-four mile limit will give border patrol agents more space and time to catch boats that might be smuggling drugs or people into the country. The bill follows international ocean rules that America already agreed to follow.

bills.short_title: Accountability for Terrorist Perpetrators of October 7th Act
bills.plain_summary: This law makes the President put special punishments called sanctions on a terrorist group called the Popular Resistance Committees and other similar groups within 90 days. bills.title: STRONG Support for Children Act of 2025

bills.plain_summary: The STRONG Support for Children Act of 2025 creates two programs that give money to help kids who have been through scary or harmful experiences. The first program gives money to state and local health departments so they can use computer information to find places where many children have had bad experiences and help connect their families to services that can help them. The second program gives money to local governments and tribes so they can work together to provide care services for young children and their families who might experience trauma. bills.plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules for American companies that make payments to foreign business partners. Right now, these companies might have to pay extra taxes on those payments. bills.title: Saving NASA’s Workforce Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill protects NASA workers from being laid off or fired until Congress approves NASA's complete budget for 2026. NASA can still fire employees if they do something seriously wrong or are not doing their job well. The law is meant to keep NASA workers safe from losing their jobs while Congress decides how much money NASA will get. It only stops NASA from firing workers for budget reasons, not for breaking rules or poor performance.

bills.title: WATCH Act

These foreign labs that do experiments on animals must be checked four times every year to make sure they are treating the animals properly. If a lab fails these checkups and does not fix the problems, they will lose all their US government money. This law helps protect animals used in scientific research around the world.

The first program gives money to local health departments so they can better keep track of when people try to harm themselves and share this information with the government. The second program gives money to hospital emergency rooms to help them check if patients might be thinking about suicide and provide help to prevent it. Both programs work together to make communities safer by catching problems early and getting people the help they need.

bills.title: Reducing the Federal Workforce Through Attrition Act
bills.short_title: Reducing the Federal Workforce Through Attrition Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the federal government to reduce the number of people it employs by ten percent over the next few years. The government will do this by not hiring new workers to replace people who quit or retire. If a government agency has too many employees, they will not be allowed to hire anyone new until they have fewer workers. The goal is to have ninety percent of the current number of federal employees by the year 2028.

The Secretary has six months to complete the study and must focus on areas where drug traffickers commonly operate and remote communities along the coast. After finishing the study, the Secretary must share the results with Congress. The goal is to find better ways to use drone technology to keep drugs and other illegal activities from entering the United States through coastal areas.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.

bills.title: Protecting Americans from Tax Hikes on Imported Goods Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Protecting Americans from Tax Hikes on Imported Goods Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the President from using emergency powers to raise taxes on goods brought into America from other countries. It also prevents the President from setting special limits on how much of certain products can be imported. However, during an emergency, the President can still completely block all imports from specific countries if needed. The law is meant to protect Americans from higher prices on imported goods during emergencies.

bills.title: Death Tax Repeal Act of 2025

The bill changes gift tax rules so people can give away up to ten million dollars each year without paying taxes on it. These changes would help families keep more of their money when it gets passed down from one generation to the next.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for when factories and power plants need special permits under the Clean Air Act. Changes made to reduce pollution, improve safety, or make operations more reliable do not require new permits unless the EPA decides they would harm people's health or the environment. The bill also says that changes that do not significantly increase emissions do not count as major modifications that need permits.

bills.short_title: No Taxpayer Funding for Abortion and Abortion Insurance Full Disclosure Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes permanent rules that stop the federal government from using taxpayer money to pay for abortions or health insurance plans that cover abortions. The only exceptions are when the pregnancy is from rape or incest, or when the mother's life is in danger. The bill also stops federal employees and federal hospitals from providing abortions, and it requires insurance companies to clearly tell customers if their health plans cover abortions and how much extra it costs. These rules would apply to all federal money, including money used by Washington DC.

bills.title: Dental Care for Veterans Act
bills.plain_summary: The Dental Care for Veterans Act would let all veterans who get health care from the VA also get dental care from the VA. Right now, most veterans can only get VA dental care if they have tooth problems caused by their military service or meet other special rules. The new law would change this over four years, with different groups of veterans getting dental care at different times based on things like their disabilities or how much money they make. This means many more veterans would be able to get their teeth fixed through the VA instead of having to pay for it somewhere else.

bills.title: Saving the Civil Service Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the government from creating Schedule F jobs, which are a type of government position that can be easily fired. It makes sure that most government workers keep their job protections and cannot be moved to different types of positions without agreeing to it first. The bill also limits how many workers can be moved from protected jobs to unprotected jobs each time there is a new president. This protects government employees from being fired just because of their political beliefs.

bills.short_title: Unrecognized Southeast Alaska Native Communities Recognition and Compensation Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps five Alaska Native villages in Southeast Alaska that were left out when land was given to other Native communities years ago. The villages are Haines, Ketchikan, Petersburg, Tenakee, and Wrangell. Each village can now form a special corporation and will receive about 23,000 acres of federal land. The Native people living in these villages can become owners in their new corporations while still keeping their rights in the larger regional corporation. These new corporations can also create trusts to help their people with health, education, and preserving their Native culture.

The warning must tell people that the guidance is not a real law and cannot be enforced like a law. bills.title: Rural 340B Access Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill allows rural emergency hospitals to buy medicines at lower prices through a special government program called 340B. Rural emergency hospitals are small hospitals in country areas that mostly help people in emergencies and let them stay for less than 24 hours. To join this program, these hospitals must be owned by the government, be nonprofit organizations with government powers, or be private nonprofits that agree to help poor patients who don't have Medicare or Medicaid. The 340B program makes drug companies sell medicines at cheaper prices to hospitals that help low-income people.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.subjects_str: Health care costs and insurance, Health care coverage and access, Hospital care, Prescription drugs, Rural conditions and development
bills.plain_keywords_str: rural hospitals, cheap prescription drugs, emergency hospitals, country hospitals, drug discounts, 340B program, lower medicine prices, small town hospitals, rural healthcare, affordable medications

bills.title: Birthright Citizenship Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for who automatically becomes a US citizen when they are born in America. Under the new rules, a baby born in the US would only become a citizen if at least one parent is a US citizen, a legal permanent resident, or a non-citizen legally serving in the military. Right now, almost all babies born in the US automatically become citizens regardless of their parents' status. The new rules would not change the citizenship of anyone who was already born before this law takes effect.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_keywords_str: birthright citizenship, babies born in America, automatic citizenship, citizenship rules, born in US citizen, parent citizenship requirements, immigration citizenship law, who becomes US citizen, birth citizenship changes, citizen by birth, newborn citizenship, immigration reform

bills.title: 911 SAVES Act
bills.plain_summary: The 911 SAVES Act says the government must change how 911 operators are classified in their job system. Right now, these workers are not listed as protective service workers, but they should be because they save lives during emergencies. The bill recognizes that 911 operators do much more than just answer phone calls. The government has 30 days after this bill becomes law to make this change happen.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

bills.title: Chiropractic Medicare Coverage Modernization Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Chiropractic Medicare Coverage Modernization Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes Medicare to cover more chiropractic services than it does now. Currently, Medicare only pays for chiropractors to adjust the spine, but this bill would let Medicare pay for any service that chiropractors are allowed to do in their state. Chiropractors would need to take a one-time online training class to provide these new services, but they can keep doing spine adjustments without taking the class. This gives older Americans on Medicare access to more types of chiropractic care.

bills.title: Repealing Big Brother Overreach Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would get rid of a law called the Corporate Transparency Act that was passed in 2021. That law requires companies to report information about who really owns them to the government. The government uses this information to help stop terrorism funding and money laundering. If this new bill passes, companies would no longer have to give the government this ownership information. The bill would also clean up other laws by removing mentions of the canceled act.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the names of two places at Paterson Great Falls National Historical Park in New Jersey. The Great Falls Scenic Overlook Trail Bridge will be renamed the Bill Pascrell, Jr. Scenic Overlook Trail Bridge. The Overlook Park will be renamed Bill Pascrell, Jr. Overlook Park. These changes honor Congressman Bill Pascrell, Jr. by adding his name to these park areas.

bills.title: To amend the Aquifer Recharge Flexibility Act to clarify a provision relating to conveyances for aquifer recharge purposes.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier to use underground water storage projects on federal land. People who already have permission to transport water across government land can now also use that same permission to store water underground in natural underground areas called aquifers. They can do this for themselves or help states, tribes, and public groups do it too. They just need to tell the government 30 days ahead of time and do not have to pay extra fees unless they are making money from it.
bills.last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

bills.plain_summary: This bill gives an official name to a famous mountain in Wyoming. The mountain is located at Devils Tower National Monument and will now be called Devils Tower. The government must use this name in all of its papers and records when talking about the mountain and the area around it. This makes Devils Tower the official name that everyone in the government has to use.

bills.title: Cattle Fever Tick Eradication Program Enhancement Act
bills.short_title: Cattle Fever Tick Eradication Program Enhancement Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the Department of Agriculture to hire a college or university to study a program that fights cattle fever ticks. These ticks spread a dangerous disease to cattle called bovine babesiosis or cattle fever. The study must look at how well the program works to stop the disease, how it affects cattle farmers, what treatments are used, and how much money the government spends on it. The college must then write a report with their findings and suggestions for making the program better and give it to Congress within two years.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Livestock, Dairy, and Poultry.

bills.title: ACRES Act
bills.plain_summary: The ACRES Act requires two government departments to create better reports about work they do to prevent wildfires on federal lands. These departments must count how many acres of land they worked on each year and check if their fire prevention work actually helps reduce wildfire risk. They also need to create standard ways to track and verify this information to make sure it is accurate. The reports will be shared with Congress as part of the budget process, and another government office will study how well this new system works.
bills.last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.

bills.title: CBW Fentanyl Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives the President new powers to punish foreign countries when their government workers help make or spread chemical weapons, biological weapons, or chemicals used to make fentanyl that harm other countries. The President must start with lighter punishments like stopping trade with that country's chemical companies, then move to stronger punishments like ending aid to that country, and finally blocking all money dealings with them if they don't fix the problem. The President can temporarily skip these punishments if it's really important for America's safety, and must stop the punishments when the foreign country fixes what they did wrong. The goal is to force countries to stop their governments from being involved in making dangerous chemicals and biological weapons that hurt people in other countries.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.
bills.plain_keywords_str: fentanyl, chemical weapons, biological weapons, foreign countries, trade sanctions, drug manufacturing, international punishment, government aid, dangerous chemicals, country penalties, fentanyl production, weapons sanctions

bills.title: Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025 helps the government better handle wildfires from start to finish. The bill creates special emergency teams to help areas right after fires happen and sets up money to fix damaged land and buildings over time. It also speeds up getting new technology like sensors and cameras to spot fires early, gives money to Native American tribes for firefighting equipment, and funds research on using drones to fight fires. The law requires the government to use the best science when planning how to manage fires and makes sure states get paid back when military training accidentally causes fires.
bills.last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported with an amendment in the nature of a substitute favorably.

bills.title: Overturn Biden’s Offshore Energy Ban Act
bills.short_title: Overturn Biden’s Offshore Energy Ban Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill cancels two orders that President Biden made in January 2025 that stopped oil and gas companies from getting permits to drill underwater in federal waters. The orders covered large areas in the Gulf of Mexico, Atlantic Ocean, Pacific Ocean, and Bering Sea. By canceling these orders, the bill would allow the government to give out permits for oil and gas drilling in these ocean areas again. The original orders prevented a government agency called the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management from letting companies explore for oil and gas or drill in these underwater areas.

bills.title: Cost-Share Accountability Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Cost-Share Accountability Act of 2025 makes the Department of Energy report to Congress about cost-sharing in energy projects. Usually, when the government funds energy research and development projects, the people or companies getting the money have to pay for part of the costs themselves. Sometimes the Department of Energy lets them pay less or nothing at all. This law requires the Department of Energy to tell Congress every three months when they reduce or eliminate these cost-sharing requirements and explain why they made those decisions.

bills.title: Energy for America’s Economic Future Act
bills.short_title: Energy for America’s Economic Future Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a special fund to help pay down the national debt. Every three months, the government must put 25% of the money it makes from selling oil and gas leases into this fund. The government must also put 25% of money it makes from artificial intelligence projects into the same fund. All this money must be used to buy back government bonds, which helps reduce how much the country owes.

bills.short_title: Law Enforcement Officer and Firefighter Recreation Pass Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates free passes for law enforcement officers and firefighters to visit national parks and other federal recreation areas. These workers would get the same type of free annual passes that military members already receive. To get a free pass, law enforcement officers and firefighters must show proof that they work in these jobs. The passes would allow them to visit national parks and federal recreation lands without paying the usual entrance fees.

bills.title: Alaska Native Vietnam Era Veterans Land Allotment Extension Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Alaska Native Vietnam Era Veterans Land Allotment Extension Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps Alaska Native veterans who served in the Vietnam War get land they deserve. These veterans can receive up to 160 acres of federal land in Alaska, but they had to apply by December 29, 2025. The bill gives them five more years to apply, moving the deadline to December 29, 2030. This extra time helps make sure more eligible veterans and their families can get the land benefits they earned through their military service.

bills.title: Wildfire Prevention Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires federal agencies like the Forest Service to do more work to prevent wildfires on government land. They must cut down more trees, do more controlled burns, and use cattle grazing to reduce fire risks, with goals to increase these activities by 40% by 2029. The bill also creates a program to test new wildfire technology with private companies and makes it easier to do this work without long environmental reviews. Local governments and tribes can now join court cases about forest projects that help prevent fires or involve cutting timber.

bills.title: MAPWaters Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The MAPWaters Act of 2025 requires the Forest Service and Department of the Interior to create online databases that show the public where they can use federal waterways for boating, fishing, and other water activities. These agencies must work together to make sure their computer systems can share information easily and that all the data follows the same standards. Within five years, they must put maps and information online showing which waterways people can access, what restrictions exist, and where fishing is allowed or not allowed. The agencies must update boating and waterway information at least twice a year and update fishing rules right away when they change. The public will also be able to ask questions or make comments about the information provided.
bills.last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-62.

bills.title: Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a program run by the Department of the Interior to help replant trees and fix damaged land after disasters like wildfires and storms. The program will focus on federal lands and Indian lands that cannot recover naturally without help. Each year, the department will make a list of the most important areas that need to be fixed first. They can give out grants and contracts to organizations to do the work and make sure there are enough seeds and baby trees available for replanting.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for communities in Colorado to pay for a big water pipeline called the Arkansas Valley Conduit. bills.title: EARA
bills.plain_summary: This law helps people get quicker decisions when they disagree with the Department of the Interior about how land can be used. When someone appeals a decision to the Interior Board of Land Appeals, they can ask for their case to be handled faster. If they make this request, the board must make a final decision within six months. If the board does not meet this deadline, the person can take their case straight to court without waiting any longer.

bills.plain_summary: The CONTAINER Act lets border states put up temporary movable barriers on federal land near the borders with Canada and Mexico. States can do this without asking for special permission first. The barriers can stay for up to one year to help with border security. If border security goals are not met, the barriers can be extended for additional 90-day periods.

The volunteer groups must be people who did not cause the pollution in the first place and have not broken any mining rules. This program will run until 2032 to help restore damaged areas from old coal mining.

bills.plain_summary: This law requires the government to hold oil and gas lease sales in the Gulf of Mexico twice a year for ten years. Each sale must offer at least 74 million acres of ocean area for companies to drill for oil and gas. The law also bans new drilling in areas close to Florida's coast and other sensitive ocean areas until 2035. Companies that already have leases in those banned areas can keep them, and some areas can be used for environmental protection projects instead.

bills.title: Historic Roadways Protection Act
bills.plain_summary: This law stops the Bureau of Land Management from spending money to close old historic roads on public land in Utah. These roads were built long ago to help people settle in the American West, and Utah and its counties are fighting in court to keep them open. The law says the government cannot make or carry out plans that would close these roads until all the court cases are finished. This protects the historic roads from being shut down while the legal fights are still going on.

bills.title: Protecting American Energy Production Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the President from banning hydraulic fracturing across the entire country unless Congress passes a law that allows the ban. Hydraulic fracturing is a way to get oil and gas from underground by pumping water, sand, and chemicals into rocks to crack them open. The bill says that state governments should be the main ones in charge of making rules about hydraulic fracturing on state and private land. This means states would have more control over this type of energy production in their areas instead of the federal government.

bills.title: Repeal the NFA Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would get rid of a law called the National Firearms Act. The National Firearms Act is a tax law that controls certain types of guns and requires people to pay special taxes on them. The bill would remove this entire law from the tax code. If this bill passes, the government would no longer collect these special gun taxes or have these gun rules.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Veterans Member Business Loan Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps military veterans get business loans from credit unions. Right now, credit unions can only make a certain amount of business loans before hitting their limit. Under this new law, when credit unions make business loans to veterans, those loans will not count toward that limit. This means credit unions can make more business loans to veterans without worrying about going over their usual lending limits. The law will start working six months after it gets signed.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

bills.title: No Retaining Every Gun In a System That Restricts Your Rights Act
bills.short_title: No Retaining Every Gun In a System That Restricts Your Rights Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about gun store records when those stores close down. Right now, when a gun store goes out of business, it has to give all its records about gun sales to the federal government. This bill would stop that from happening in the future. It also requires the government to destroy all the gun store records it has already collected within 90 days. The government must then report to Congress about how many records were destroyed.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: PURR Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The PURR Act of 2025 gives the FDA complete control over pet food rules for dogs and cats across the whole country. This means states and cities can no longer make their own different pet food laws, which should make things less confusing for pet food companies. The FDA must review new pet food ingredients within 90 days, and companies can use ingredients that are already considered safe without getting special permission first. Pet food companies can also make certain claims about their products, like saying they are natural or good for pet health, without asking the FDA first as long as they meet certain rules.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: SAVE Act
bills.plain_summary: The SAVE Act requires people to show documents that prove they are US citizens when they register to vote in federal elections. States must check that voters are citizens and remove anyone who is not a citizen from their voter lists. The bill lists specific documents that count as proof of citizenship, like certain types of ID cards. If election workers register someone without proper proof of citizenship, they could face criminal charges or be sued.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

bills.title: _______ Act of 2024
bills.plain_summary: This bill says that before the President or Defense Secretary can order National Guard troops to work full-time in any state, they must first get permission from that state's governor. If the National Guard needs to work in Washington DC, they must get permission from the mayor instead. The bill also makes sure these National Guard activities follow an old law that stops the military from acting like police officers. This rule applies to all US states, territories, and Washington DC.

bills.title: Repeal the TikTok Ban Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill removes a law that was made to control apps owned by countries that are enemies of the United States. The old law put restrictions on certain apps and websites to keep them from operating in America. This new bill takes away all of those restrictions and lets those apps work freely again. It completely gets rid of the rules that were blocking foreign-owned apps from being used in the United States.

bills.title: AMERICANS Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill protects military members who were kicked out or punished for not getting COVID-19 vaccines. It makes sure these service members get honorable discharges and allows them to come back to the military with full back pay. The bill also stops the military from treating unvaccinated members unfairly and says they must get the same chances for jobs and promotions as everyone else. Finally, it prevents the military from requiring COVID-19 vaccines in the future unless Congress specifically says it is okay.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

bills.title: Travel Trailer and Camper Tax Parity Act
bills.short_title: Travel Trailer and Camper Tax Parity Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules for businesses that sell travel trailers and campers. Right now, businesses can get special tax breaks when they borrow money to buy cars and trucks to sell in their stores. But they cannot get the same tax breaks when they borrow money to buy travel trailers and campers for their stores. This bill would let businesses that sell travel trailers and campers get the same tax benefits as businesses that sell regular cars and trucks.

bills.title: Protect the Gig Economy Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Protect the Gig Economy Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new rule for federal courts that makes it much harder for workers to sue gig economy companies like Uber or DoorDash. The rule says that workers cannot join together in group lawsuits if they want to argue that the company should have treated them as employees instead of independent contractors. Workers would have to sue these companies one by one instead of working together. This protects gig companies from facing large group lawsuits about how they classify their workers.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Protect Funding for Women’s Health Care Act
bills.short_title: Protect Funding for Women’s Health Care Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop the government from giving money to Planned Parenthood and any organizations connected to it. The bill says that other doctors and health clinics will still help women with their health care needs. The same amount of government money will still be available for women's health services, but it will go to these other health care providers instead of Planned Parenthood. Congress believes women will still be able to get the health care they need from different places.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: TEAM Veteran Caregivers Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes changes to how the Department of Veterans Affairs handles caregiver programs for veterans. It requires the VA to put the names of all veteran caregivers in the veteran's health records so they are officially recognized. The VA must also send clear letters to veterans and caregivers explaining any decisions about their benefits, like if benefits are reduced or ended. When veterans lose eligibility for comprehensive caregiver benefits, they can keep getting those benefits for at least 90 more days, unless there are special reasons like fraud or the caregiver moving away.

bills.title: Veteran Overmedication and Suicide Prevention Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Veteran Overmedication and Suicide Prevention Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This law tells the Department of Veterans Affairs to hire the National Academies to study veteran suicides from the past five years. The study must look at veterans who got medical care from the VA during the five years before they died. It will count how many veterans died by suicide, violent death, or accidental death and collect information about these veterans. The study will also look at what medications and treatments these veterans received and report the results to Congress and the public.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

bills.title: Recover Fraudulent COVID Funds Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about how long the government has to go after people who stole money from COVID relief programs. Right now, the government usually has five years to catch and punish people who committed fraud with pandemic relief money. This bill would give the government ten years instead of five years to find these criminals and take them to court. The longer time limit would apply to all the major COVID relief programs that gave money to businesses and people during the pandemic. This means the government would have more time to investigate and recover money that was stolen from these programs.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: FAIR Act
bills.plain_summary: The FAIR Act gives federal employees a pay raise in 2026. Most federal workers will get a 3.3 percent increase in their basic pay. All federal employees will also get an extra 1 percent increase in their locality pay, which is based on where they work. This means federal workers will earn more money to help keep up with rising costs.

bills.short_title: Veterans Affairs Transfer of Information and Sharing of Disability Examination Procedures With DOD Doctors Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how military members get checked for disabilities when they leave the military. If a service member might have a disability that could get them VA benefits, they must be examined by a doctor who is certified by the VA. The VA has to accept whatever that certified doctor decides about the disability. The bill also requires the military and the VA to work together to create a shared computer system that keeps track of medical records and other important information about service members and veterans.

bills.title: Retired Pay Restoration Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for military veterans who get both retirement pay and disability pay. Right now, veterans with disabilities rated less than 50% must give up some of their retirement pay when they get disability money. This bill would let all disabled veterans keep both their full retirement pay and their full disability pay at the same time. The change would apply to veterans who served at least 20 years or were forced to retire early because of their disability.

bills.title: Stopping Adversarial Tariff Evasion Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for where imported goods come from when the government takes action against unfair trade. If a company from an enemy country like China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, or Venezuela owns or controls a business that makes products in another country, those products will be treated as if they came directly from the enemy country. This means companies cannot avoid trade penalties by simply moving their factories to different countries while still being controlled by adversary nations. The new rule helps prevent countries from getting around trade enforcement by using other countries as middlemen.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

bills.title: Promoting Resilient Buildings Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for local governments to get money from FEMA to make buildings safer during disasters. Local governments can now use FEMA funding to follow either the newest building safety rules or the ones right before them, instead of only being able to use the very newest rules. The bill also creates a new program that helps regular homeowners get money to make their houses stronger against floods, storms, and other disasters. This homeowner program will last until 2028 and can use up to 10 percent of the money that FEMA gives out each year.
bills.last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 273.

bills.title: Back to Work Act
bills.plain_summary: The Back to Work Act limits how often federal government workers can work from home to no more than 40 percent of their work days. Workers who work from home will have their pay reduced because they will not get certain pay raises that other workers get. The bill requires agencies to watch workers from home using computer monitoring and review their work agreements every year. Government agencies must report to Congress each year about how well working from home is going and what problems they are having. Some workers like military spouses or those with special jobs may be allowed to work from home more often.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.title: Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act
bills.short_title: Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act
bills.plain_summary: This law requires the Secretary of the Interior to give about 40 acres of land at the Wounded Knee Massacre site in South Dakota to two Native American tribes within one year. The land will become a protected memorial and sacred site that belongs to the Oglala Sioux Tribe and Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe together. The tribes will own the land under special rules that prevent them from selling it and protect it from state and local taxes. The land can only be used for purposes that both tribes agree on, and no gambling or other commercial businesses are allowed there.
bills.last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-61.

bills.title: No Taxpayer Funding for Abortion and Abortion Insurance Full Disclosure Act of 2025
bills.short_title: No Taxpayer Funding for Abortion and Abortion Insurance Full Disclosure Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes a permanent rule that the federal government cannot use taxpayer money to pay for abortions or health insurance plans that cover abortions. The only times this rule does not apply is when a woman becomes pregnant from rape or incest, or when her life is in danger and she needs an abortion to save her life. The bill also says that federal buildings and federal workers cannot provide abortions. Additionally, health insurance companies must clearly tell people if their plans cover abortions and how much extra that coverage costs.

bills.title: A bill to require agencies submit zero-based budgets.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires all federal government agencies to create special budgets called zero-based budgets every six years. When making these budgets, agencies must look at all their programs from the beginning and decide which ones are most important. They also have to suggest ways to spend at least two percent less money than they did the year before. The Department of Defense and the National Nuclear Security Administration do not have to follow the rule about cutting spending.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes prosecutors in big cities report information about serious crimes to the federal government every year. The crimes include murder, rape, robbery, assault, burglary, theft, car theft, arson, and gun crimes. This rule only applies to cities with 380,000 or more people that get certain federal money. bills.plain_summary: The Security First Act gives money to help secure the US border with Mexico from 2025 to 2028. The bill uses money taken from people who try to cross the border illegally with unreported cash to fund a program called Operation Stonegarden, which helps local police work on border security. bills.plain_summary: The PRECEPT Nurses Act creates a tax credit to help train more nurses. bills.title: Improving Federal Assistance to Families Act
bills.short_title: Improving Federal Assistance to Families Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new way to measure poverty that takes into account how much things cost in different states. The government will compare this new measurement to the current poverty line and use whichever one shows more people are poor in each state. This means more families could qualify for government help programs like food stamps or healthcare. The bill also asks for a study of another poverty measurement called ALICE to see if it might work better for government programs.

bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new rule that anyone found guilty of rape or sexual assault in federal court must go to prison for at least 30 years. bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a rule to control government spending on programs that are not for defense or the military. Starting in 2026, if the government spends more than one percent extra on these programs compared to the last year, the extra money above one percent will be automatically cut. The cuts will be spread equally across all non-defense programs. This means the government cannot increase spending on things like education, healthcare, and other services by more than one percent each year without automatic cuts happening.

These new rules would apply to cases going back to 2017.

bills.title: No Bailout for Sanctuary Cities Act
bills.short_title: No Bailout for Sanctuary Cities Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from giving money to cities and states that don't share information about immigrants with federal immigration officials. These places are called sanctuary jurisdictions because they have rules that limit working with immigration agents or sharing details about people's citizenship status. If a city or state refuses to hold immigrants for federal agents or tell them when someone is released from jail, they cannot get federal money that would be used to help people who are in the country illegally. The Department of Homeland Security must make a yearly list of places that don't cooperate with immigration requests.

bills.title: Expression of Interest Sensibility Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes when people have to pay fees for asking the government to sell them rights to drill for oil and gas on federal land. Right now, people pay a fee just for asking, even if the government never actually sells the land. Under this new bill, people only pay fees if they win the auction to lease the land, or if they were the first to ask about land that nobody else wanted to buy. bills.short_title: Protecting Homeowners from Disaster Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules to help homeowners who lose property in disasters. Right now, people can only deduct disaster losses on their taxes if the government declares it a federal disaster. Starting in 2025, this bill removes that limit so homeowners can deduct any personal property losses from disasters like fires, floods, or storms on their taxes. This means more families will be able to get tax help when they lose their belongings in disasters, even if the government doesn't officially call it a federal disaster.

The law applies to all types of agency rules and guidance documents. This means courts will have more power to overturn agency decisions they disagree with.

bills.title: Whole Milk for Healthy Kids Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Whole Milk for Healthy Kids Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about what kinds of milk schools can serve in their lunch programs. Schools will now be able to offer whole milk, reduced-fat milk, low-fat milk, and fat-free milk to students, and all of these can be flavored or unflavored and organic or regular. The bill also says that when schools count saturated fat in meals, they don't have to count the fat that comes from milk. Schools are not allowed to buy milk from companies owned by the Chinese government, and parents can now write notes asking for milk substitutes for their children instead of only doctors being able to do this.

bills.plain_summary: This bill is about three men who are accused of planning the September 11th terrorist attacks. bills.title: Write the Laws Act
bills.plain_summary: The Write the Laws Act would stop Congress from letting other parts of government make rules that act like laws. Instead, Congress would have to write all the laws themselves with specific details. The bill says that any new laws, rules, or decisions made 90 days after this bill passes must follow this requirement or they will not count. A government office would have to find all the old laws that give rule-making power to agencies and report them to Congress.

bills.title: Ending Catch and Release Act of 2025

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes immigration laws to require that most people seeking asylum or arriving without proper papers be held in detention centers instead of being released while they wait for their court hearings. bills.title: Censorship Accountability Act
bills.plain_summary: The Censorship Accountability Act lets people sue federal government workers who violate their free speech rights. If a government employee stops someone from speaking or expressing themselves when they should be allowed to under the First Amendment, that person can take the employee to court. The law applies to most government workers except the President and Vice President. If someone wins their case, the court can make the government worker pay their lawyer fees.

bills.plain_summary: This congressional resolution says that states have the right to keep out people who are not supposed to be there. It claims that from 2021 to 2024, border states along Mexico were invaded by terrorists, drug cartels, and criminals. The resolution argues that these states have the constitutional power to defend themselves against this invasion without waiting for the federal government. It includes many statistics about illegal border crossings and crimes to support its argument that the Biden administration failed to protect these states.

bills.title: Panama Canal Repurchase Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives the President permission to talk with Panama's government about buying back the Panama Canal. The Panama Canal is an important waterway that the United States used to own but gave to Panama years ago. The President must tell Congress within six months how these talks are going, what problems might come up, and what could happen. The goal is to see if America can get control of the Panama Canal again.

bills.title: Mens Rea Reform Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for federal crimes by requiring the government to prove what was going on in a person's mind when they committed a crime. When a law does not say what mental state is needed to convict someone, the government must now prove the person knew what they were doing was wrong. This applies to all federal crimes written in laws and government rules. The bill creates a standard rule that says people must have acted knowingly to be found guilty of crimes where the mental requirement is not clearly stated.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules for people who want to buy or make special types of guns like machine guns and silencers. If someone applies to get one of these guns, the government has 90 days to say yes or no. If the government does not answer in 90 days, the person automatically gets approved. The bill also lets people appeal if their application gets denied and requires the government to write reports about background check delays.

bills.title: Small Business Regulatory Flexibility Improvements Act
bills.short_title: Small Business Regulatory Flexibility Improvements Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules to help protect small businesses from government regulations that could hurt them. Before government agencies create new rules, they must study how those rules might affect small businesses and look for ways to make the rules less harmful. Agencies also have to ask small businesses for their opinions before making major rules and regularly check if old rules are still causing problems. The bill makes it easier for small businesses to take agencies to court if they don't follow these protection rules, and it stops agencies from fining small businesses the first time they make paperwork mistakes.

bills.title: Strategic Production Response and Implementation Act
bills.short_title: Strategic Production Response and Implementation Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the government cannot take oil out of the Strategic Petroleum Reserve unless it first agrees to lease more federal land for oil and gas drilling. The amount of new land that must be leased has to match the percentage of oil being taken from the reserve. However, the government can still use the reserve oil during serious energy emergencies without leasing new land. The total increase in leased federal lands cannot be more than 10 percent.

bills.title: Washington, D.C. Admission Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make Washington D.C. into the 51st state called Washington Douglass Commonwealth. Most of D.C. would become the new state, but important government buildings like the White House, Capitol, and Supreme Court would stay as a separate federal area called the Capital. The new state would get to elect two senators and one representative to Congress just like other states. A special group would help make sure the change from being a federal district to becoming a state goes smoothly.

bills.plain_summary: The COLLUDE Act makes big changes to the laws that protect websites from being sued for content posted by users. Right now, websites like Facebook and Twitter usually can't be blamed for what other people post on their sites. bills.title: Federal Agency Sunset Commission Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Federal Agency Sunset Commission Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a group called the Federal Agency Sunset Commission that will decide which government agencies should be shut down. The Commission has thirteen members who must look at every government agency at least once every twelve years to see if it is still needed and working well. If the Commission decides an agency should be closed, Congress must vote to keep it running or it will automatically shut down. The Commission will also check if government agencies are doing things they are not supposed to do and will tell Congress about any new agencies that lawmakers want to create.

bills.short_title: Build Housing with Care Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Build Housing with Care Act of 2025 creates a new program that gives money to help build housing near child care centers. This means families could live close to where their children get care during the day. The bill also asks the government to study how well families in public housing can find and pay for child care. The goal is to make it easier for parents to find both a place to live and child care in the same area.

bills.title: Lumbee Fairness Act
bills.plain_summary: The Lumbee Fairness Act gives full federal recognition to the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina by changing a 1956 law. This means the Lumbee people can now get all the same services and benefits that other federally recognized tribes receive. The government can also hold land in trust for the tribe, which helps protect their territory. North Carolina will continue to handle legal matters on tribal land unless the state and tribe agree to change this arrangement.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

bills.title: Sustainable Budget Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a special group of 18 people to study the government's money problems and find ways to fix them. The group would have members picked by the President and Congress leaders from both political parties. They need to come up with a plan to balance the government's budget within 10 years and deal with programs like Social Security and Medicare that cost a lot of money. If enough members agree on the plan, the President must ask Congress to vote on it using special rules that make the voting process faster.
