bills.title: Methane Border Adjustment Mechanism Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new tax on oil and natural gas that comes from other countries. The tax is based on how much methane pollution those countries make when producing the oil and gas. The goal is to encourage other countries to pollute less and help American clean energy companies compete better. The law also requires the US to work with other countries to create rules for tracking and reducing methane pollution.

bills.title: LASSO Act
bills.plain_summary: The LASSO Act is a law that takes some money from federal lands and puts it toward Social Security. Every year, the government collects money from activities like drilling, mining, and logging on public lands owned by the federal government. This new law says that 10 percent of that money must go into the Social Security Trust Fund to help pay benefits to retired people and their families. The law makes sure that agencies cannot raise their prices or take money away from states and local communities to make up for this change.

bills.title: Expressing the sense of the House of Representatives that the Federal Government should drop all charges against Edward Snowden.
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the House of Representatives thinks the government should drop all criminal charges against Edward Snowden. Snowden shared secret information about a government program that collected phone records from many Americans. The House believes this phone records program was against the law and that Snowden helped the public by exposing it. The government charged Snowden with crimes for sharing classified information and stealing government property, but the House thinks these charges should be dropped.

bills.title: Hawaii Invasive Species Protection Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the government to carefully check all people, bags, packages, and mail going to or from Hawaii to stop harmful plants and animals from getting to the islands. The checks will happen at airports, shipping ports, and mail centers using visual searches, x-ray machines, and specially trained dogs. The government will work with Hawaii to make a list of dangerous items to watch for. People and businesses will have to pay fees to cover the cost of these inspections.

bills.title: HUD Transparency Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This law makes the inspector who watches over the Department of Housing and Urban Development come to Congress every year to give a report. The inspector must testify by October 1st each year. During this meeting, the inspector has to talk about how they are stopping fraud and waste at the department. They also have to explain if they have enough resources to do their job and give ideas for making the department work better.

bills.title: SHOW UP Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The SHOW UP Act of 2025 makes federal government workers go back to the same work-from-home rules they had before the coronavirus pandemic started in 2020. All government agencies have thirty days to change their policies back to what they were on December 31, 2019. If an agency wants to let more employees work from home than before the pandemic, they must get special permission and prove it will help their work and save money. The bill also requires agencies to study how the increase in remote work during the pandemic affected their operations.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.title: Coordination for Soil Carbon Research and Monitoring Act
bills.short_title: Coordination for Soil Carbon Research and Monitoring Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new government committee to help different federal agencies work together on studying soil and how it can store carbon. The committee will be run by the Office of Science and Technology Policy and will make plans and reports for Congress about soil carbon research. The goal is to learn how soil can help fight climate change by storing carbon from the air. The committee will focus on including farmers and communities that have not had much help before in this research.

bills.title: Advanced Border Coordination Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Advanced Border Coordination Act of 2025 requires the Department of Homeland Security to build at least two special centers along the border between the United States and Mexico. These centers will help different law enforcement groups work together better, including federal agents, state police, local police, and tribal officers. The centers will help coordinate efforts to stop illegal activities like drug smuggling, human trafficking, and people crossing the border illegally. The Department of Homeland Security must set up these centers within six months of the bill becoming law.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.

bills.title: Defund Planned Parenthood Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from giving money to Planned Parenthood for one year. Planned Parenthood can only get federal money if they promise not to do abortions, except in cases of rape, incest, or when the mother's life is in danger. The bill gives 235 million dollars to community health centers instead so women can still get health care. These community health centers must follow the same rules about abortions as Planned Parenthood.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Complete COVID Collections Act
bills.plain_summary: The government gave out lots of money during COVID-19 to help people and businesses. Some people took this money when they should not have or lied to get it. This new law says the government must work harder to get back money that was stolen or given to the wrong people. The government will have more time to catch these people and take them to court. Any money they get back will be used to help pay off the country's debt.

bills.title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to establish a refundable tax credit for individuals for amounts paid for gas and electricity for primary residences.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new tax credit that helps people pay for their gas and electricity bills at their main home. People can get up to three hundred fifty dollars back each year if they qualify. To get this money, you must earn less than two hundred thousand dollars per year if you are single or four hundred thousand dollars if you are married and filing taxes together. If you rent your home and pay for utilities as part of your rent, your landlord must report how much of your rent goes toward gas and electricity. You cannot use this credit if someone else claims you as a dependent on their taxes or if you already get other tax breaks for the same utility costs.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Interstate Commerce Simplification Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about when states can tax businesses from other states. Right now, states cannot tax out-of-state businesses if they only ask customers to buy things and then ship those things from outside the state. The bill makes it easier for businesses to avoid state taxes by including more business activities under the protection from taxes. This means more out-of-state businesses might not have to pay taxes to states where they sell their products.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Knife Owners’ Protection Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill protects people who travel between states with knives. If you own a knife legally in one state and want to take it to another state where it is also legal, you can transport it without getting arrested. You must store the knife safely while traveling, unless it is an emergency knife for cutting seat belts. States and cities cannot arrest you if you follow these rules. If someone tries to arrest you anyway, you can use this law to defend yourself in court.
bills.last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 13 - 10.

bills.title: Ensuring Accountability in Agency Rulemaking Act
bills.short_title: Ensuring Accountability in Agency Rulemaking Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how government agencies make new rules. Any new rule that goes through the normal public comment process must be started by a high-ranking official who was chosen by the President. The rule must also be signed by someone the President picked and the Senate approved. There are only a few exceptions for emergencies involving public safety or security. This is meant to make sure important officials are responsible for new government rules.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.plain_summary: The MAPLE Act changes a government program that helps low-income seniors buy food. The program gives seniors special coupons to shop at farmers markets and local farm stands. bills.title: To terminate the designation of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan as a major non-NATO ally, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_summary: This bill removes Pakistan's special status as a major non-NATO ally of the United States right away. Being a major non-NATO ally gives countries special benefits like getting extra military supplies from America and working together on defense projects. Pakistan can only get this special status back if the President says Pakistan has done enough to fight against a terrorist group called the Haqqani Network. Pakistan would need to attack this group, stop them from using Pakistan as a safe place to hide, work with Afghanistan to control their shared border, and arrest the group's leaders.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

bills.title: Federal Grant Accountability Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for how much money colleges and universities can charge the federal government for extra costs when doing research projects. Right now, when the government gives money for research, schools can charge extra fees to cover things like building costs and office staff that help with the research but are not part of the actual project. The bill says schools can only charge the government the same amount they charge private companies for these extra costs. The government will study how much schools currently charge for these costs and write a report about it within one year.

bills.plain_summary: The RIFLE Act of 2025 changes how the government oversees gun dealers and stores. The bill makes it harder for the government to punish gun dealers by requiring officials to work with dealers to fix problems before taking away their licenses. bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Treasury to make special coins in 2028 to honor the women who worked in factories and other jobs during World War Two. bills.title: Defending American Sovereignty in Global Pandemics Act
bills.short_title: Defending American Sovereignty in Global Pandemics Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the United States cannot join any World Health Organization agreement about pandemics unless two-thirds of the Senate votes to approve it as a treaty. The bill also stops all US government money from going to the World Health Organization starting when any pandemic agreement takes effect. The money would only start flowing again after the Senate approves the agreement. The bill is meant to make sure Congress has a say before the US joins international health agreements.

bills.title: Washington, D.C. Admission Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make Washington D.C. into the 51st state called Washington Douglass Commonwealth. The new state would include most of the current D.C. area, but important government buildings like the White House, Capitol, and Supreme Court would stay as a separate federal area called the Capital. The new state would get to send two senators and one representative to Congress just like other states. Current D.C. laws and court cases would continue under the new state government, and a special group would help make the change from D.C. to statehood go smoothly.

bills.title: LICENSE Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The LICENSE Act of 2025 makes it easier to get a commercial driver's license by changing the testing rules. The bill lets more people give the written test to truck drivers, including anyone who already has the right training and certification. It also lets any state give the driving test to commercial license applicants, even if they live in a different state. The Transportation Secretary has ninety days to update these rules to make the whole process work better.

bills.short_title: Keweenaw Bay Indian Community Land Claim Settlement Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives $33.9 million to the Keweenaw Bay Indian Community in Michigan. The money pays them back for land that the federal government took from their reservation without paying for it. In return, the tribe agrees to give up all legal claims to those lands. Current landowners will get clear ownership of their property. The tribe can use the money for anything legal except buying land for casinos.

bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the government from spending any money to close or move the Marine Corps training base at Parris Island in South Carolina. The government also cannot use money to plan for closing or moving the base. Parris Island has been training Marines since 1891 and the bill wants to keep it open. bills.title: Secret Service Prioritization Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes what jobs the Secret Service does by moving most of their crime investigation work to the FBI. The Secret Service will keep doing their main job of protecting the President, Vice President, and other important government leaders, plus investigating threats against these people. The FBI will take over investigating counterfeiting money, computer crimes, identity theft, and other financial crimes that the Secret Service used to handle. This change will happen within thirty days to help the Secret Service focus only on keeping government leaders safe.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules to help companies build cell towers and internet equipment faster. It stops state and local governments from unfairly blocking these projects or taking too long to approve them. The government must make decisions within set time limits, and if they don't respond in time, the project gets approved automatically. Companies that think they are being treated unfairly can ask courts to step in quickly. The goal is to help build better internet and phone service across the country.

bills.title: National Human Trafficking Database Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a national online database to track human trafficking cases across the United States. The Office for Victims of Crime will give money to state law enforcement agencies to collect information about trafficking cases, court cases, and groups that help victims. All this information will be put together in one place online and updated every year. The database will help people understand how big the human trafficking problem is while keeping victims' personal information private and safe.

bills.title: Freedom for Families Act
bills.plain_summary: The Freedom for Families Act makes it easier for people to use Health Savings Accounts to help their families. Right now, you can only have these accounts if you have a expensive health insurance plan, but this bill would let anyone open one. The bill also lets people take money out of these accounts without paying extra taxes when they need to take time off work to care for a new baby, sick family member, or deal with their own serious health problems. Finally, the bill increases how much money people can put into these accounts each year from about $4,300 to $9,000 for single people and from about $8,550 to $18,000 for families.

bills.title: Skinny Labels, Big Savings Act
bills.plain_summary: The Skinny Labels, Big Savings Act protects generic drug companies from being sued for patent violations when they use special labels called "skinny labels." These labels let generic companies sell their drugs by only listing the medical uses that are no longer protected by patents, while leaving out the uses that still have patent protection. A court case made it risky for companies to use skinny labels because they could still get sued, even when following the rules. This new law makes it clear that generic and biosimilar drug companies cannot be sued for patent violations when they properly use skinny labels approved by the FDA. This protection helps keep generic drugs available and affordable for patients.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: No Budget, No Pay Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop members of Congress from getting their paychecks if they don't do their job of making a budget. Both the House and Senate would need to agree on a budget plan and pass all the spending bills for the year before any member could get paid. If they don't finish this work, they won't get any money during that time and can't get that money back later. The rule would start in 2027.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the tax rates that people pay on estates, gifts, and generation-skipping transfers. bills.title: To eliminate certain limitations and exclusions regarding defense articles and requirements regarding security assistance and sales with respect to the Republic of Cyprus.
bills.plain_summary: This bill removes special rules that made it harder for the United States to sell weapons and provide military help to Cyprus, Greece, and Turkey. Right now, the President has to prove to Congress every year that these countries are doing certain things before the US can help them with defense. For Cyprus, the President has to show they are fighting money laundering and not helping Russian military ships. For Greece and Turkey, the President has to prove that giving them weapons will keep peace in the area. This bill gets rid of all those extra requirements so the US can treat these countries like other allies.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

bills.title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to prohibit Members of Congress from receiving compensation during a fiscal year unless both Houses of Congress have agreed to a concurrent resolution on the budget for that fiscal year prior to the beginning of that fiscal year.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to change the Constitution so that members of Congress cannot get their paychecks unless they pass a budget on time. Both the House and Senate would need to agree on the budget before the new fiscal year starts, or else no one in Congress gets paid. To make this change to the Constitution, two thirds of Congress must vote yes and then three fourths of all state governments must also approve it. This rule would start working the year after it becomes part of the Constitution.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: COVER Now Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a program that lets cities and counties provide health insurance to low-income adults in states that chose not to expand Medicaid. The federal government will pay all the costs for the first three years and most costs after that. Local governments can run this program for up to 10 years or until their state decides to expand Medicaid. The bill also stops states from punishing cities and counties that join this program by cutting their funding or raising their taxes.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Improving Federal Financial Management Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes changes to how the government handles money and keeps track of spending. It requires the Office of Management and Budget to create a four-year plan for improving how all government agencies manage their finances and report this plan to Congress. The bill also gives Chief Financial Officers at government agencies more jobs to do, like overseeing budgets, managing risks, and making sure their agencies follow the government-wide financial plan. The goal is to make the federal government better at managing taxpayer money and keeping accurate records of how it is spent.

bills.title: Keep Every Extra Penny Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Keep Every Extra Penny Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop the government from taxing overtime pay. Right now, when workers earn extra money for working more than 40 hours a week, they have to pay federal income tax on that overtime money. If this bill becomes law, workers would get to keep all their overtime pay without paying federal income tax on it. This means workers who work extra hours would take home more money.

bills.title: Prescription Freedom Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop the Food and Drug Administration from deciding which medicines need a doctor's prescription. Instead, each state would get to make those rules for themselves. The change would happen six months after the bill becomes law. However, the FDA would still control prescription rules for drugs that end pregnancies. This means states could allow people to buy many medicines without seeing a doctor first.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.plain_summary: This bill stops members of Congress, their spouses, and children from buying or selling individual stocks and similar investments. If they break these rules, they have to pay fines and cannot claim tax losses on those investments.

bills.title: Laken Riley Act
bills.plain_summary: The Laken Riley Act says that the government must arrest and hold people who are not US citizens if they have been charged with crimes like stealing, burglary, or shoplifting. These people must be held even if they are just charged with these crimes and not yet found guilty. The law also lets state governments sue the federal government if they think immigration rules are not being followed properly and this causes harm to their state. States can sue if the harm costs them more than one hundred dollars. This gives states more power to make sure immigration laws are enforced the way they want.
bills.last_action_text: Read the second time. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 10.

bills.title: FAIR PREP Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the IRS from preparing tax returns for people, including through their Direct File program that lets some taxpayers file for free on the IRS website. The IRS can still help people through existing programs like Free File, where people use other companies' software for free, and volunteer programs that help low-income people with their taxes. The Treasury Department also cannot spend money or make deals to create new tax preparation services. The bill basically says the IRS should not be in the business of preparing tax returns for taxpayers.

bills.title: SHARKED Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The SHARKED Act of 2025 creates a special team to solve problems with sharks taking fish off fishing lines before fishermen can catch them. This team will include fishing experts, scientists, and people from state agencies who will work together for up to seven years. They will study the problem, find ways to reduce conflicts between sharks and fishermen, and create educational materials to help people understand the issue. The team must report their findings to Congress every two years to show what they have learned and what solutions they recommend.

bills.plain_summary: This bill would allow computer programs that use artificial intelligence to give patients prescriptions for medicine. Right now, only licensed doctors and other medical professionals can write prescriptions for drugs. Under this new law, AI computer programs could also write prescriptions, but only if they get approval from both the state government and the federal Food and Drug Administration first. bills.title: REMOTE Act
bills.plain_summary: The REMOTE Act requires government agencies to track how much their employees use computer networks and login systems, whether they work from home or in the office. Agencies must collect this data and compare how often remote workers and office workers log in to their computers each day. When a manager stops letting an employee work from home, they must write down exactly why they made this decision and what other steps they tried first. The government must also report if working from home is causing any problems with how well agencies do their jobs.

bills.title: Federal Freeze Act
bills.plain_summary: The Federal Freeze Act stops the government from giving pay raises to federal workers for one year. It also stops agencies from hiring new people during that year, except for jobs in law enforcement, public safety, or national security. After the first year, government agencies must start reducing their number of workers. By the end of three years, each agency must have five percent fewer employees than when the bill started.

bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from using taxpayer money to put in place new rules about Salmonella bacteria in raw chicken and turkey. The Department of Agriculture proposed these rules in August 2024 to make chicken and turkey safer by testing for harmful bacteria and keeping better records. The new rules would say that chicken and turkey with too much Salmonella bacteria cannot be sold in stores. This bill would prevent those safety rules from happening by not allowing the government to spend money on them.

bills.title: A bill to exclude locality adjustments from average pay for purposes of computing the amount of retirement annuities of new employees.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how retirement benefits are calculated for new federal workers. Many federal employees get extra pay called locality adjustments if they work in areas where private sector jobs pay more than government jobs. Right now, this extra pay is included when calculating how much money federal workers will get in retirement. Under this new law, only federal employees hired after the law passes would have their retirement benefits calculated without counting this extra locality pay. This means their retirement benefits would likely be lower than under the current system.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.title: Fair Representation Amendment
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells fifteen different House committees to look at all the laws they work with and find places where the Executive Branch has too much power to make decisions on their own. Each committee has six months to suggest changes that would give Congress more control over how laws are used. After that, the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform must write a new bill called the Article One Restoration Act that includes all of these suggestions. The goal is to make sure Congress has more say in how laws work instead of letting the Executive Branch decide everything.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

bills.plain_summary: The Federal Employee Return to Work Act stops federal workers from getting yearly pay raises if they work from home one or more days per week. bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it much easier for workers to join unions and negotiate with their bosses for better pay and working conditions. bills.title: A resolution providing for sufficient time for legislation to be read.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution creates a new rule for how long the Senate must wait before voting on bills. For every 20 pages in a bill, senators must wait at least one day before they can vote on it. If a bill has extra pages that don't add up to 20, they still have to wait one more day for those pages too. Any senator can object if they think the Senate is trying to vote too quickly without enough waiting time. The only way to skip this waiting period is if three-fifths of all senators vote to override the rule.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration. (text: CR S80)

bills.title: Protecting our Land Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the President must make new rules to stop certain foreign countries from buying land or buildings in America. The rules would block countries that are enemies of the United States or countries that help terrorists from purchasing any real estate. The bill covers all states and territories in America. The President would tell government agencies to create and enforce these new rules to protect American land from being bought by unfriendly foreign governments.

bills.title: Establishing the Select Committee on Electoral Reform.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a special 14-member committee in Congress to study how we elect members of the House and Senate. The committee will look at different ways to vote, like ranked choice voting where you pick your first, second, and third choices, having multiple representatives from each district, and changing how primary elections work. They will listen to experts and write a report with their ideas within one year. The goal is to see if different voting methods could make Congress work better for Americans.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

bills.title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to provide for balanced budgets for the Government.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to add a new rule to the Constitution that says the federal government cannot spend more money than it takes in each year. The President would have to create a budget that does not spend more than the government receives. Congress could only break this rule if two-thirds of both the House and Senate vote to allow it, or if there is a war, natural disaster, or national emergency. This would force the government to balance its budget like families have to do with their money.

bills.title: Assuring Medicare’s Promise Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps keep Medicare funded by creating new taxes on wealthy people's investments. People who make more than $400,000 per year if single or $500,000 per year if married will have to pay a special tax on money they make from investments and businesses. All the money collected from this tax will go directly to Medicare's trust fund to help pay for healthcare for older Americans. This means Medicare will have more money to keep providing medical care to seniors.

bills.title: STEP Act
bills.plain_summary: The STEP Act makes government agencies work harder to stop wrong payments and fraud. Agencies must watch new programs that spend more than $100 million closely for their first four years. They have to get their boss's approval for how they guess wrong payment amounts and write detailed reports about stopping fraud. Agencies must send reports to Congress every year for ten years about how they are fighting fraud and fixing money problems. The law does not give agencies extra money to do this work.

bills.plain_summary: This bill requires two government departments to share information about people who entered the US illegally. Every week, the Department of Homeland Security and Department of Health and Human Services must post updates on their websites about these individuals. The updates must include how many people were caught each day, what countries they came from, their ages and genders, where they are going in the US, and if they have committed crimes before. bills.title: EMPSA
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps married adults with intellectual or developmental disabilities get fairer treatment when applying for SSI benefits. Currently, when these adults get married, the government counts their spouse's money and income when deciding if they can get SSI, which often means they lose their benefits. This bill changes that rule so the government will only look at the disabled person's own money and income, not their spouse's. This means adults with these disabilities can get married without worrying about losing the financial help they need.

bills.title: NOSHA Act
bills.plain_summary: The NOSHA Act would shut down OSHA, which is the government agency that makes sure workplaces are safe for employees. OSHA creates safety rules for businesses and checks that companies follow them to protect workers from getting hurt or sick on the job. This bill would eliminate all of OSHA's work, including safety training and education programs. It would also get rid of the 1970 law that first created workplace safety rules. If this bill passes, there would no longer be a federal agency making sure American workers have safe places to work.

bills.title: Balanced Budget Accountability Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would hold back Congress members' pay if they don't pass a balanced budget by April 16th each year. A balanced budget means the government can't spend more money than it brings in, and spending can't be more than 18% of what the country produces. The bill also says that any vote to raise taxes or fees must get approval from three-fifths of Congress members, not just a simple majority. For the current Congress, members who miss the deadline would have their pay held until they pass a budget, but starting with future Congresses, they would only get paid one dollar for the rest of that year.

It praises Pro Women's Healthcare Centers as a good example of the kind of care women should receive. The resolution wants these same high standards to be used across the entire country. The goal is to make sure women everywhere can get compassionate and convenient healthcare services.

bills.title: Rural Telehealth and Education Enhancement Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Rural Telehealth and Education Enhancement Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill keeps a government program running until 2030 that helps people in rural areas get better access to healthcare and education. The program is run by the Department of Agriculture and gives money to rural communities so they can buy technology and get training. With this technology, people in rural areas can see doctors remotely and take classes online without having to travel long distances. The program makes it easier for people living in the countryside to get medical care and education that people in cities can access more easily.

bills.title: To prohibit the implementation of the Approved Resource Management Plan Amendment for the Buffalo, Wyoming Field Office of the Bureau of Land Management.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the government from using a new land management plan for federal land around Buffalo, Wyoming. The new plan was created after a court said the government had to study how coal mining affects the environment. The updated plan, released in November 2024, stops any new coal mining leases on this land to reduce pollution, but allows existing coal mines to keep working. This bill would prevent that new plan from being used.

bills.title: Nuclear Waste Informed Consent Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the Department of Energy cannot spend money from the Nuclear Waste Fund to build places to store dangerous nuclear waste unless they get written permission first. They must get agreements from state governors, local governments, nearby towns that trucks would drive through, and Indian tribes that would be affected. Everyone listed must agree in writing before any money can be spent. If anyone wants to change the agreement later, everyone must agree to the changes.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Sunshine Protection Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Sunshine Protection Act of 2025 would make daylight saving time permanent across the United States. This means we would stop changing our clocks twice a year in spring and fall. Instead, we would keep the same time all year long that we currently use during daylight saving time. States that do not currently observe daylight saving time in some areas could choose what time to use in those places.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

bills.title: STAND Act
bills.plain_summary: The STAND Act stops the government from spending money on foreign aid for 60 days after the President declares a major disaster in the United States. This includes money that goes to help other countries with things like humanitarian aid and development programs. The idea is to focus government spending on helping Americans during disasters first. Congress can vote to override this freeze and allow foreign aid to continue if they think it is necessary.

bills.title: Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says that if a baby is born alive during an abortion, doctors and nurses must give the baby the same medical care they would give any other newborn baby. They must also take the baby to a hospital right away. If healthcare workers don't follow these rules, they could go to jail for up to five years. If someone kills a baby that was born alive, they can be charged with murder. The mother cannot be punished under this law, but she can sue if healthcare workers break these rules.
bills.last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.plain_summary: This bill is about stopping illegal tunnels that people dig under the US border. The government already has a plan to find and stop these tunnels, but they only had to write one report about it. Now this bill says they must write a report every single year about how well their plan is working. The reports will help Congress know if the government is doing a good job stopping the illegal tunnels.

bills.title: Protect Minors from Medical Malpractice Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Protect Minors from Medical Malpractice Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill lets people sue doctors for up to 30 years if those doctors gave them gender-transition treatments like hormones or surgery when they were under 18 years old. Gender-transition treatments are medical procedures that change someone's body to match a different gender than they were born with. The bill says no federal law can force doctors to do these treatments on minors. If a state makes doctors perform these treatments on kids, that state will lose federal health money. The bill does not apply to treatments for certain medical conditions or life-threatening emergencies.

bills.title: Paws Off Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Paws Off Act of 2025 makes a new rule about foods that contain xylitol. Xylitol is a sweetener that can be very dangerous and even deadly to dogs if they eat it. Under this new law, any food with xylitol in it must have a warning label that tells people it is toxic to dogs. The Food and Drug Administration has six months to one year to create the rules for how these warning labels should work. This law is meant to help keep dogs safe by making sure pet owners know which foods could hurt their pets.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for people who miss their immigration court hearings. Right now, if someone doesn't show up to court and gets ordered to leave the country, they can't get certain types of help with their immigration case for 10 years. This bill would make that ban last forever instead of just 10 years. The only way someone could get help after missing court would be if something very unusual happened that wasn't their fault.

bills.title: No Budget, No Pay Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says that Congress members will not get their paychecks if their part of Congress does not pass a budget by April 15th. Their pay will be held starting April 16th until they pass a budget or until their time in Congress ends. The bill applies to both the House and Senate separately, so each part must pass their own budget to get their pay back. This rule is meant to make Congress members work harder to pass budgets on time.

bills.title: Federal Employee Performance and Accountability Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Federal Employee Performance and Accountability Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a test program for five years that changes how some federal workers get paid based on how well they do their jobs. Workers at higher levels who do really well can get up to 10% more pay, workers who do okay get no extra pay, and workers who do poorly get 10% less pay and extra training. Each government agency has to pick between 1% and 10% of their eligible workers to try this new system. Agencies have to report each year on how well the program is working, and at the end there will be a final report on whether it was successful.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.title: Noncontiguous Shipping Competition Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the Jones Act, which normally requires that only American-built ships with American crews can carry goods between US ports. The new law would allow foreign ships to carry goods to Hawaii, Alaska, Puerto Rico, and other US territories if there isn't enough competition from American shipping companies. Foreign ships could only be used when fewer than three American companies serve a route, or when those companies don't each handle at least 20 percent of the goods on that route. The goal is to increase shipping competition and potentially lower costs for people living in these areas.
bills.last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR E90-91)

People must get this notice at least six months before their passport runs out. The notice will tell them when their passport expires and explain how to get a new one. It will also tell them where they can turn in their renewal paperwork. The government can send this notice by email or regular mail.

bills.title: Affordable Shipping for All Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes shipping companies charge the same prices to send packages to Alaska, Hawaii, and US territories like Puerto Rico as they charge to send packages anywhere in the other 48 states. Shipping companies also cannot refuse to deliver to these places. The rule applies to both private companies like FedEx and the US Postal Service. However, very expensive items that cost more than ten thousand dollars do not have to follow these rules.

bills.title: DIRECT Act
bills.plain_summary: The DIRECT Act takes leftover money that was supposed to go to the IRS for tax enforcement and gives it to border security instead. The money must be used by U.S. Customs and Border Protection to hire new border agents and officers. These new workers will help secure the southern border between the United States and Mexico. This bill moves the unused IRS money from the Inflation Reduction Act to border protection activities.

bills.title: Prohibition on IOER Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops Federal Reserve banks from paying interest to regular banks on extra money they keep stored there. Right now, banks can earn interest on all the money they keep at Federal Reserve banks, even if it's more than they are required to keep. Under this new law, banks would only earn interest on the minimum amount of money they must keep at Federal Reserve banks by law. Any extra money beyond that minimum would not earn any interest.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

bills.title: FAITH in Small Business Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for religious organizations and faith-based groups to get business loans and disaster help from the government. Right now, these groups often cannot get this help if their main job is teaching about religion. The bill would change the rules so that faith-based organizations can get the same business loans and disaster money as other small businesses. This change comes after court cases said it was unfair to deny help to groups just because they are religious.

bills.title: Regulation Decimation Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires government agencies to get rid of ten old rules before they can make any new rule that costs money or creates requirements for people, businesses, or local governments. If the new rule would cost more than 100 million dollars per year, the agency must make sure the ten old rules they remove cost at least as much as the new rule. The bill does not apply to rules about how agencies run themselves, government purchasing, or rules that are being changed to make them easier to follow. All agencies must also write reports to Congress listing their rules and saying which ones are too expensive, don't work well, repeat other rules, or are too old.

bills.title: Child Care Workforce and Facilities Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Child Care Workforce and Facilities Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a grant program run by the Department of Health and Human Services to help areas that don't have enough child care options. The grants will go to states and tribal governments to pay for half the cost of training child care workers or building and fixing up child care centers. The goal is to make sure more families can find good child care in places where it's hard to find. States and tribes have to apply for the money and show how they will use it to help their communities.

bills.title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to limit the number of terms that a Member of Congress may serve.
bills.plain_summary: This bill proposes changing the Constitution to limit how long people can serve in Congress. Under the new rules, a person could only serve up to six years in the House of Representatives, which equals three terms. They could only serve up to twelve years in the Senate, which equals two terms. The limits would only apply to terms that start after the amendment becomes law. To become part of the Constitution, three-fourths of all state governments would need to approve this change within seven years.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Increased TSP Access Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how the government finds and approves people who help farmers with conservation practices like protecting soil and water. It allows non-government groups to certify these helpers instead of just the government doing it, and creates faster approval for people who already have special training. The bill also says these helpers must be paid the same amount as government workers who do similar jobs. Finally, the government must share information with the public about how well this program is working and how much it costs.

bills.title: HONDURAS Act
bills.plain_summary: The HONDURAS Act gives the President power to stop sending money and help to Honduras if that country forces American military workers and other US staff to leave Soto Cano Air Base. This would happen if Honduras refuses to let American personnel stay at the base. If the President uses this power, Honduras would lose all aid from the United States, including military help that America has been giving them since 1954.

bills.plain_summary: This bill says that states must teach people how to use trailers safely as part of their highway safety programs. The programs need to cover important topics like what safety equipment trailers need and how to take care of them properly. bills.title: SAGA Act
bills.plain_summary: The SAGA Act stops states and cities from making stricter rules about rifles and shotguns than the federal government has. This means local governments cannot charge higher taxes or fees on these guns or create tougher penalties than federal law allows. If states or cities already have stricter gun laws for rifles and shotguns, those laws would no longer work under this bill. The law is meant to make sure federal rules are the strongest rules that can apply to rifles and shotguns anywhere in the country.

bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Secretary of Agriculture to write a report about help for Texas farmers. Mexico is supposed to give water to the United States because of an agreement made in 1944, but Mexico has not been doing this. Texas farmers have lost money because they did not get the water they were supposed to get. bills.title: Keep Your Coins Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Keep Your Coins Act of 2025 stops government agencies from preventing people from using digital money like Bitcoin for their own spending. The law also protects people's right to keep their digital money in their own personal wallets that they control themselves. This means the government cannot make rules that stop you from buying things with cryptocurrency or from storing your digital coins in your own wallet instead of letting a company hold them for you.

bills.plain_summary: Congress is using this resolution to cancel a new rule from the Department of Energy. The rule would have required businesses to use more energy-efficient walk-in coolers and freezers. The Department of Energy said these new standards would save the most energy possible while still being affordable and technically possible. However, Congress decided to reject this rule and prevent it from becoming law. This means businesses will not have to follow the new energy-saving requirements for their walk-in coolers and freezers.

bills.title: Yosemite National Park Equal Access and Fairness Act
bills.short_title: Yosemite National Park Equal Access and Fairness Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes a 1913 law that let San Francisco build a dam in Yosemite National Park. The bill makes San Francisco pay much more rent each year, going from $30,000 to $2 million, for using the park's water areas. The money will help pay for preventing wildfires in California's national parks. The bill also requires the National Park Service to let more people use these water areas for fun activities like swimming and boating while still protecting the natural beauty of the park.

bills.title: Fire Department Repayment Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Fire Department Repayment Act of 2025 requires four government departments to create standard rules for paying local fire departments when they help fight wildfires on federal land. The Departments of Agriculture, Interior, Homeland Security, and Defense must make these rules within one year and review all current agreements to make sure they follow the new standards. The rules must make sure that fire departments get paid back quickly when they submit proper bills for helping fight fires. This bill aims to help local fire departments get their money faster when they assist with fires that spread across different areas owned by federal and local governments.

bills.plain_summary: This law lets the military sell old aircraft and aircraft parts to groups that fight wildfires. The aircraft can drop water or special chemicals on fires to put them out. bills.title: Offshore Lands Authorities Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill cancels eight orders made by presidents that stopped oil and gas companies from drilling in certain ocean areas. It also creates new rules that limit how much power future presidents have to block drilling in offshore waters. Under the new rules, presidents can only withdraw smaller areas for shorter amounts of time, and they must do detailed studies first. Congress will also get the chance to vote against any presidential order that tries to stop offshore drilling.

bills.title: HITS Act
bills.plain_summary: The HITS Act is a law that helps people who make music recordings in the United States save money on their taxes. If someone spends money to record music, they can subtract up to $150,000 of those costs from their taxes right away each year. The law also gives special tax breaks called bonus depreciation benefits to people who make sound recordings. bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules about which non-citizens can come to or stay in the United States. If a non-citizen has been convicted of or admits to crimes like stalking, child abuse, sex crimes, domestic violence, or breaking protection orders, they cannot enter the country. bills.title: Action Versus No Action Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how government agencies study the environmental effects of forest management projects on public lands. Instead of looking at many different ways to manage the forest, agencies can only study two choices: doing the proposed forest work or doing nothing at all. This rule applies to forest projects that involve cutting trees and are in areas with insect problems, disease issues, or wildfire risks. When agencies study the "do nothing" option, they must look at how this might affect forest health, wildfire danger, and threats to people's lives and property.

bills.title: Neighbors Not Enemies Act
bills.plain_summary: The Neighbors Not Enemies Act removes an old law called the Alien Enemies Act from U.S. law books. This old law allowed the President to arrest and kick out people from other countries if the United States was at war with their home country or if their country threatened to invade America. The bill gets rid of four parts of this old law. By removing this law, the government would no longer have these special powers to remove foreign citizens during wartime.

bills.title: SWAMP Act
bills.plain_summary: The SWAMP Act would force most federal government agency headquarters to move out of the Washington DC area. States and local governments could compete to have these agencies move to their areas by making proposals. Agencies that are already in Washington could stay but would not be allowed to build new buildings, make major repairs, or sign new leases for office space. bills.plain_summary: The Protect and Serve Act of 2025 makes it a federal crime to attack police officers and other law enforcement workers. bills.plain_summary: This bill says the American flag must be flown at its highest point on every presidential Inauguration Day. bills.title: Natural Disaster Recovery Program Act of 2025

bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new disaster recovery program to help states and tribal governments pay for things they still need after major disasters happen. It makes it easier for areas to get declared as disaster zones by having FEMA look more closely at local impacts and recent disaster history. bills.plain_summary: This bill lets people take money out of their retirement accounts early without paying extra taxes if they have been unemployed for at least 26 weeks. People can only take out up to $50,000 per year, and the amount also depends on how much money is in their retirement accounts. bills.title: Saving the Civil Service Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from moving workers out of the competitive hiring system without good reasons. Federal workers in the competitive system have to take tests and meet requirements to get their jobs, and they also have more protection from being fired unfairly. The bill says agencies cannot move workers to different job categories without the worker agreeing to it first. It also limits how many workers can be moved each time there is a new president. The goal is to protect federal workers from losing their job protections when new presidents take office.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for getting the child tax credit on your taxes. To get this tax credit, parents must provide social security numbers for themselves and their children, but only certain types of social security numbers count. The social security numbers must be given to people who are allowed to work in the United States. Military families get special treatment under this rule. If someone doesn't provide the right social security number, the IRS will treat it as a math error on their tax return.

bills.plain_summary: This congressional resolution supports an international agreement called the Geneva Consensus Declaration that focuses on women's health and family issues. The declaration says there is no international right to abortion and that families are very important to society. President Biden removed the United States from this agreement, but Congress wants America to rejoin it. The resolution also says the US should not pay for or support abortions or abortion lobbying in other countries.

bills.title: Sarah’s Law
bills.plain_summary: This law says that immigration officials must arrest and hold immigrants who are in the country illegally and have been charged with crimes that killed someone or badly hurt someone. The officials have to keep these immigrants locked up instead of letting them go free. The law also requires officials to find out who the victims were and tell the victims or their families about the immigrant who hurt them. Officials must share information like the immigrant's name, age, where they are from, and whether they are being sent out of the country.

bills.title: ROOMIE Act
bills.plain_summary: The ROOMIE Act requires federal government agencies to bring most of their workers back to the office full-time. At least 80 percent of employees must work in their office buildings Monday through Friday, and agencies must use at least 60 percent of their office space. If an agency does not have enough workers to fill 60 percent of its building, it must make a plan to share space with other agencies or find more employees. Agencies that cannot meet these rules must sell their buildings or end their rental agreements.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.plain_summary: This bill would change the Constitution to limit when the President can pardon people. The President would not be allowed to pardon themselves, their family members, people who work in their administration, or paid campaign workers. The President also could not pardon anyone who committed crimes to help the President or because the President told them to. Any pardons given for corrupt reasons would not count. Congress would have the power to make sure these new rules are followed.

bills.title: Chinese Currency Accountability Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This law says the United States must vote against giving China's money more power in a special international money system run by the International Monetary Fund. The US can only vote yes if China follows all the rules, stops manipulating its currency, and agrees to work fairly with other countries on loans and trade credits. The International Monetary Fund uses a basket of different countries' money to create special reserve money that countries can use in emergencies. China has to prove it is playing by international financial rules before its currency can get more influence in this system. This law will stop working automatically after ten years.
bills.last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

bills.title: NO GOTION Act
bills.plain_summary: The NO GOTION Act is a law that stops certain foreign companies from getting tax breaks for green energy projects. These companies are ones that are owned by or controlled by China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea. The government will not give these companies special tax benefits that help pay for things like solar panels or wind farms. This rule starts for any tax years that begin after the law is passed. The goal is to prevent these specific countries from benefiting from American green energy tax programs.

bills.title: Drug Testing for Welfare Recipients Act
bills.short_title: Drug Testing for Welfare Recipients Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes states test adults for illegal drugs before they can get government help like food stamps, cash assistance, or housing aid. Adults who were arrested for drug crimes in the past five years must take a drug test, while others take a screening test first and only get drug tested if they seem at high risk. Anyone who fails a drug test cannot get benefits for at least one year until they complete drug treatment and pass a new test. Their family members can still get help even if the adult is denied benefits. States that do not follow these rules will get less money from the federal government.

bills.plain_summary: This bill helps people who were hurt by Hurricanes Helene and Milton by giving them tax breaks. bills.title: LNG Public Interest Determination Act of 2025

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how the government decides whether to allow companies to export natural gas to other countries. Instead of one agency making these decisions, the Department of Energy will now be in charge and must make sure exports won't make climate change much worse, raise energy prices too much for Americans, or hurt the health of poor and minority communities. The new location in Texas must be picked to help deal with problems at the border with Mexico. bills.title: Family First Act
bills.plain_summary: The Family First Act changes how families pay taxes by giving them bigger tax credits for children. Parents can get up to $4,200 for each child and pregnant women can get up to $2,800 for unborn babies that are at least 20 weeks old. The bill also makes some tax rules simpler by getting rid of certain filing options and deductions that families used before. However, it keeps the limit on how much people can deduct for state and local taxes forever instead of letting it end in 2026.

bills.plain_summary: This bill would make the President add a 10% tax on all products that come into the United States from other countries. bills.title: HEADWAY Act
bills.plain_summary: The HEADWAY Act changes the rules for teachers in Early Head Start programs that help very young children. Right now, all teachers must have finished their Child Development Associate training before they can teach. This new law would let some teachers work in classrooms while they are still learning and getting their training. Each classroom would need at least one teacher who has finished all their training, and the other teachers would get help from a mentor while they learn. bills.title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
bills.short_title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops three federal agencies from banning lead ammunition and fishing tackle on federal lands where hunting and fishing are allowed. The agencies that cannot make these bans are the Fish and Wildlife Service, the Bureau of Land Management, and the Forest Service. There are only two exceptions to this rule. bills.title: RED TAPE Act
bills.plain_summary: The RED TAPE Act says that when government agencies make new rules, they can only count benefits that have a clear dollar amount. Agencies cannot use benefits that are hard to put a price on, like cleaner air or safer neighborhoods. If an agency uses these non-dollar benefits anyway, courts must throw out their rule completely. The bill also requires agencies to publish their cost and benefit studies for everyone to see. This law would start working 30 days after Congress passes it.

These companies can only get paid after they prove they actually fixed someone's credit problems, and they must wait at least six months to show proof. bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier and faster for the Veterans Affairs Department to fire, demote, or suspend their supervisors, managers, and other employees who do poorly at their jobs or break rules. bills.title: Protecting American Energy Production Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the President from banning hydraulic fracturing, which is a way to get oil and gas from underground by injecting water and chemicals into rock. The President can only ban this drilling method if Congress first passes a law saying it's okay to ban it. The bill also says that Congress thinks state governments should be the ones making rules about hydraulic fracturing on state and private land, not the federal government. This drilling method is also called fracking and it helps produce energy in America.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for people who come to the US border from Mexico or Canada asking to stay in the country. bills.short_title: Protecting First Responders from Secondary Exposure Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for how the government can spend money from a special program that fights drug problems. The new rules let police, firefighters, and other first responders use this money to buy special equipment that protects them from dangerous drugs like fentanyl. The money can also pay for training to teach first responders how to use this safety equipment properly. This helps keep the people who help us safe when they are doing their jobs around deadly substances.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: CHILD Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The CHILD Act of 2025 lets working parents put more money into special savings accounts to pay for childcare. Right now, parents can save up to five thousand dollars per year in these accounts without paying taxes on that money. This bill doubles that amount to ten thousand dollars per year. The amount will also go up each year to keep up with rising prices. These changes will start in 2025 to help families afford expensive childcare costs.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Grant Integrity and Border Security Act
bills.short_title: Grant Integrity and Border Security Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules for people who want to get money from the government through grants. Before getting a grant, people must promise they have not helped bring people into the United States illegally or hidden people who are in the country illegally for the past 10 years. They also must promise not to do these things while they have the grant. If the government finds out someone broke these rules, they will take away the grant money. The Department of Justice must tell budget officials about people who break these immigration laws.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.title: WHO Withdrawal Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the President pull the United States out of the World Health Organization right away. The World Health Organization is a group of countries that work together on health problems around the world. The bill also says the US government cannot spend any money to be part of this group or similar health groups. It removes an old law from 1948 that let the US join the World Health Organization in the first place.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

bills.title: Securing Semiconductor Supply Chains Act
bills.plain_summary: This law tells a government program called SelectUSA to work with states to bring more foreign companies to build semiconductor factories in America. SelectUSA must ask state officials what they think about how to get more foreign investment in making computer chips. The program then has to write a report for Congress within two years about what the states said and how to get more companies to invest in American semiconductor manufacturing. The goal is to make sure America has a strong supply of computer chips by getting foreign companies to build their factories here.

bills.title: REMAIN in Mexico Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the Department of Homeland Security to bring back a policy called the Migrant Protection Protocols. Under this policy, people who come to the US-Mexico border seeking asylum or protection must wait in Mexico while their immigration cases go through the courts. The policy follows rules that were written in a 2019 memo. This means asylum seekers cannot stay in the United States while they wait for their court hearings.

bills.title: American Music Tourism Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This law tells the National Travel and Tourism Office to help promote music places and events to bring more visitors to America. The office must help both Americans and people from other countries learn about and visit music festivals, concert halls, and other important music spots in the United States. The law also says the office should promote sports and recreation activities to international visitors. Every two years, the office has to write a report to Congress about what they have done and what they learned.

bills.title: Mobile Post Office Relief Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the Postal Service send mobile post offices to areas where regular post offices have stopped working for a short time. The mobile post offices are like trucks or trailers that can provide mail services. They must arrive within three business days after the regular post office closes. The mobile units have to offer the same services as the regular post office until it opens again.

bills.title: Honor Our Living Donors Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for a government program that helps pay expenses for people who donate organs to others while they are still alive. Right now, the program only helps donors if the person getting the organ doesn't make too much money, but this bill removes that rule so donors can get help no matter how much the recipient earns. The bill also says donors can get help paying their expenses even if the person getting the organ could afford to pay those costs themselves. The government will have to write a report each year about whether the program has enough money to cover all the expenses that donors need help with.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Buzz Off Act
bills.plain_summary: The Buzz Off Act stops federal police from using drones to spy on specific American citizens or their homes. There are three times when they can still use drones to watch people. They can use drones if they get permission from a judge first, if there is a serious terrorist threat, or if the person being watched agrees in writing to let everyone see the recording. This law protects Americans from being watched by government drones without a good reason.

bills.title: Read the Bills Act
bills.plain_summary: The Read the Bills Act would make new rules for how Congress handles bills. Every bill would need to explain which part of the Constitution gives Congress the power to make that law, and bills that change existing laws must show exactly what they are changing. Before Congress can vote on any bill, it must be posted online for seven days, read out loud to all members, and each member who votes yes must promise in writing that they either listened to the whole reading or read the entire bill themselves. If these rules are broken, people can sue to stop the bill from becoming law.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

bills.title: Supporting the goals and ideals of a Juan Pablo Duarte Day.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution wants to create a special day to honor Juan Pablo Duarte, who helped start the country called the Dominican Republic. Juan Pablo Duarte was an important leader who helped his country become independent in 1844 and believed in democracy. The resolution asks for a national day in the United States to celebrate him and also to honor Dominican Americans who live here. This would be like other special days we have to remember important people and groups in our country.

bills.plain_summary: The WILLIS Act would stop the federal government from giving any money to the Fulton County District Attorney's Office in Georgia. The bill would also take back any federal money that was set aside for this office but not spent yet. bills.title: COAL Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The COAL Act of 2025 requires the government to quickly approve applications from companies that want to lease federal land to mine coal. If a company has already started the approval process, the government must finish reviewing their application and grant permission to lease the land. The bill also requires the government to give any additional approvals needed for mining to start on land that companies already lease. Finally, the bill cancels a 2016 order that stopped most new coal leases on federal land while the government reviewed its coal program.

bills.title: Strengthening Support for American Manufacturing Act
bills.short_title: Strengthening Support for American Manufacturing Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Commerce to hire an outside group to study how well the department helps American businesses with manufacturing and supply chains. The study must look at all the different offices that do this work and figure out what each one does, how well they work, and where they might be doing the same things. The group doing the study will then give suggestions on how to make these offices work better together and be more helpful to American manufacturers. The goal is to make sure the government is doing the best job possible to support American manufacturing and keep supply chains strong.

bills.title: No Subsidies for Wealthy Universities Act
bills.short_title: No Subsidies for Wealthy Universities Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for how much money wealthy universities can charge the government to cover extra costs when they get federal research grants. Universities with more than five billion dollars saved up cannot charge the government anything extra, while universities with two to five billion dollars saved up can only charge up to eight percent extra. All other universities can charge up to fifteen percent extra for these costs, which help pay for things like buildings and office support that research projects need. The government will keep track of how this money is spent and write reports about it every year.

bills.title: Close the Medigap Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Close the Medigap Act of 2025 makes it easier and fairer for people to buy Medigap insurance, which helps pay for medical costs that Medicare does not cover. The law says insurance companies must sell these policies to anyone who has Medicare, even if they have health problems. It also stops companies from charging much higher prices to older people and requires them to spend more of the money they collect on actual medical care instead of keeping it as profit. The bill also makes the Medicare website better so people can understand their insurance choices more easily.

bills.title: Sunset Chevron Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the Government Accountability Office to make a list of all federal agency rules that courts allowed to stay in place because of something called Chevron deference. Each rule on this list will get a date when it automatically expires, with the newest rules expiring first and older rules expiring 30 days later in order. The Supreme Court recently said courts should not automatically trust agency interpretations of laws like they used to under Chevron deference. Congress can also vote to cancel any of these rules without the usual time limits that normally apply.

bills.title: Armed Forces Endangered Species Exemption Act
bills.short_title: Armed Forces Endangered Species Exemption Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives the military special permission to ignore some rules that protect endangered animals and plants. It stops the government from saying that military bases are special protected areas for endangered species. The bill also lets military workers harm or kill endangered animals if it happens during military training or defense work, as long as hurting the animals was not the main goal. The military does not have to ask permission from wildlife agencies before doing activities that might hurt endangered species.

bills.plain_summary: This bill will shut down a government agency called the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry. bills.title: End Child Trafficking Now Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates new rules for adults who are not US citizens when they try to enter the country with children. These adults must prove they are related to the child by showing documents, having witnesses, or taking a DNA test. If an adult cannot prove they are related to the child and refuses to take a DNA test, they will not be allowed into the United States. The bill also makes it a crime for adults to use children they are not related to in order to get into the country, and they could go to prison for up to ten years.

bills.title: POLICE Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The POLICE Act of 2025 makes new rules about removing non-citizens from the United States. If a non-citizen is convicted of attacking or hurting a police officer, firefighter, or other first responder, they can be deported. This includes people who admit they committed these crimes even if they were not convicted in court. The Department of Homeland Security must publish a report every year that shows how many people were deported under this new law.

bills.title: To amend the Immigration and Nationality Act to waive certain naturalization requirements for United States nationals, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people who are U.S. nationals to become full U.S. citizens. U.S. nationals are people born in places like American Samoa who are connected to America but are not automatically citizens. The bill lets these people apply for citizenship without having to move to a U.S. state first, and they can skip requirements like English tests if they have lived in U.S. territories or states their whole lives. It also lets some children born to American parents in other countries become citizens by living in U.S. territories instead of having to come to the mainland United States.

bills.title: No Tax on Tips Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill would let workers who get tips not pay income tax on up to $25,000 of their tips each year. Only workers in jobs that normally get tips can use this tax break, and they must report their tips to their boss. Workers who make more than $160,000 a year cannot get this tax break. The bill also gives business owners a tax credit when they pay payroll taxes on tips given to workers at hair salons, nail shops, spas, and similar beauty businesses.
