bills.title: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill updates a 1998 law to help the government better handle harmful algal blooms and low oxygen levels in water that can hurt people, animals, and the economy. It requires government agencies to watch for these water problems, predict when they might happen, and respond quickly when they do occur in oceans, lakes, and rivers. The bill also sets aside money for research into new ways to prevent and control these dangerous water conditions. It makes sure that Native American tribes and rural communities are included in planning and response efforts. The updated law will run through 2030 and expands which government agencies are involved in solving these water quality problems.

bills.title: Veterans Infertility Treatment Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the VA provide fertility treatments to veterans who have trouble having babies and their partners. Veterans can get up to three successful rounds of in vitro fertilization or up to 10 tries, whichever happens first. The VA will also pay for travel costs when veterans or their partners need to go somewhere for treatment. Veterans can use donated eggs or sperm for their treatments if needed. bills.plain_summary: This bill lets state and local governments make their own immigration laws. These laws can punish people who break federal immigration rules, but the punishments cannot be worse than what federal law already says. bills.title: MEGOBARI Act
bills.plain_summary: The MEGOBARI Act is a law that punishes people who are hurting Georgia's democracy and preventing the country from joining Europe. The President must block visas and can freeze the money of Georgian government officials and other foreign people who are being corrupt or violent to stop Georgia from becoming more democratic. Family members of these bad actors can also be punished if they benefited from the wrongdoing. The law also requires reports about how China, Iran, and Russia are trying to influence Georgia. If Georgia makes progress toward democracy, they could get more help from the United States.

bills.title: Clergy Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives clergy members and Christian Science practitioners a chance to change their minds about paying Social Security and Medicare taxes. These religious workers were previously allowed to skip paying these taxes if it went against their religious beliefs, but that also meant they would not get Social Security or Medicare benefits when they retire. Now they have until 2029 to decide if they want to start paying these taxes so they can get benefits later. Once they make this choice, they cannot change it back. The IRS must tell these religious workers about this new option.

bills.title: Fix Our Forests Act
bills.plain_summary: The Fix Our Forests Act creates new rules for managing forests on government land to reduce wildfire risks. The law sets up special high-risk wildfire areas and creates a government center that different agencies work together to track and predict fires. It makes it faster and easier for the government to approve forest management projects by reducing paperwork and limiting how long people can challenge these projects in court. The law also supports programs to help communities prepare for wildfires, restore damaged forests, and help wildland firefighters and their families.

bills.title: VALOR Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The VALOR Act of 2025 is a law that puts tough punishments on Venezuela's current government led by Nicolas Maduro because they are not running the country fairly or democratically. The law blocks Venezuelan government money and property in the US, stops Americans from doing business with Venezuela's debt, and may cut off aid to other countries that help Maduro's government. At the same time, the law allows the US to help Venezuelan people and groups working for democracy, and it requires the government to make a plan to provide food, medicine, and other aid once Venezuela gets a fairly elected government. The punishments will be removed only when Venezuela has free and fair elections, respects human rights, frees political prisoners, and creates an independent court system.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

bills.title: Promoting New Bank Formation Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier to start new banks by giving them three years to slowly build up the money they need to keep on hand instead of having it all right away. During those three years, new banks can ask to change their business plans and regulators have to respond within 30 days. The bill also helps small rural banks by lowering how much money they need to keep compared to their loans for the first three years. Finally, it lets federal savings associations make loans to farmers, which they couldn't do as much before.
bills.last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 64.

bills.title: Reliability for Ratepayers Act
bills.plain_summary: This law changes how the Bonneville Power Administration can pay and hire its workers. The Bonneville Power Administration is a government agency that sells electricity from dams in the Northwest. The law lets them create a new pay plan that matches what similar electric companies pay their workers. This should help them keep good employees and hire new ones. They have to make this new pay plan within one year and update it every year.

bills.title: Domestic SUPPLY Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Domestic SUPPLY Act of 2025 creates a new program to help American companies that make protective equipment like masks and gloves. The government will make agreements to buy this equipment from these companies to prepare for health emergencies. Only American-owned companies that make most of their products in the United States can join this program, and by 2028 they must make all their products here. The law also says the federal government and states using federal money can only buy protective equipment made in America. Finally, the government must write a report about how the rules for protective equipment changed during COVID-19 and how those changes affected healthcare workers.

bills.title: Equal Access to Contraception for Veterans Act
bills.short_title: Equal Access to Contraception for Veterans Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps veterans get birth control without having to pay extra money for it. When veterans go to Veterans Affairs hospitals or clinics, they will not have to pay copayments for contraceptives that regular health insurance plans already cover for free. The bill makes sure veterans can access birth control at no cost or for very low prices. This gives veterans the same benefits that other people with health insurance already have when getting contraceptives.

bills.title: To amend the Act of August 9, 1955 (commonly known as the “Long-Term Leasing Act”), to authorize leases of up to 99 years for land in the Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe Reservation and land held in trust for the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah), and for other purposes
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes an old law from 1955 to give two Native American tribes in Massachusetts new powers over their land. The Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe and the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head can now make rental agreements for their reservation and trust lands that last up to 99 years. Before this change, these tribes could not make such long rental deals with their land. The bill adds these two tribes to a list of other groups that already had permission to make these long-term land leases.

bills.title: ERASER Act
bills.plain_summary: The ERASER Act requires government agencies to cancel three old rules before they can make any new rule. If the new rule is a major one that costs more than 100 million dollars per year, the agency must make sure the new rule costs the same or less than the three old rules they are canceling. The bill only applies to certain types of rules and does not cover things like employee rules or how agencies buy supplies. The Government Accountability Office must write reports about how many rules exist and how much they cost.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the government from using a new land management plan for public lands in southwestern Wyoming. The plan was published in December 2024 and covers areas in five counties including Lincoln, Sweetwater, Uinta, Sublette, and Fremont. The bill says the Bureau of Land Management cannot put this plan into action or enforce any of its rules. This means the government office that manages these public lands cannot use this particular plan to make decisions about how to take care of the land.

bills.title: Constitutional Concealed Carry Reciprocity Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Constitutional Concealed Carry Reciprocity Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill would let people with permits to carry hidden guns in their home state also carry those guns in other states that allow hidden guns. The person must follow the rules of the state they are visiting. If someone stops them from carrying their gun when they should be allowed to, they can take that person to court. The bill does not let people carry guns in places where the owners say no guns are allowed.

bills.short_title: Protecting the Dignity of Unborn Children Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it a federal crime to carelessly throw away or abandon fetal remains in landfills or waterways. Fetal remains means any part of a fetus after an abortion, except for cremated parts. People who break this law can be fined or sent to prison for up to three years. bills.title: SEC Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The SEC Act of 2025 changes how financial regulators count violations when companies break securities laws. Instead of counting each separate wrongful act as its own violation, regulators must group together violations that come from the same cause, false statement, or ongoing problem. All violations in each group get treated as just one violation when deciding penalties. This means companies may face smaller fines because multiple related violations will only count as one violation instead of many separate ones.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

bills.title: To establish new ZIP Codes for certain communities, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the U.S. Postal Service that it must create new ZIP Codes for 14 communities in different states across the country. Each of these communities will get their own special ZIP Code that no other place uses. The Postal Service has 270 days to make these new ZIP Codes after the bill becomes law. The communities are located in states like California, Connecticut, Colorado, Florida, Iowa, Wisconsin, and Wyoming.
bills.last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.title: READY Accounts Act
bills.plain_summary: The READY Accounts Act creates special savings accounts that help people prepare for disasters and fix their homes afterward. People can put up to $4,500 each year into these accounts without paying taxes on that money. The money in the account grows without being taxed, and people can take it out tax-free to pay for things like stronger windows, better roofs, or repairs after storms and fires. If someone uses the money for anything else, they have to pay taxes and a penalty on it.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Justice for Victims of Sanctuary Cities Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Justice for Victims of Sanctuary Cities Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill lets victims of serious crimes sue local and state governments that have sanctuary city policies if those policies helped a non-citizen criminal avoid being caught by federal immigration officers. To win the lawsuit, the victim must show that the local government did not cooperate with federal requests to hold or notify them about releasing the criminal. Local governments that take certain federal grant money cannot claim they are protected from these lawsuits. The bill also protects local officials from being sued if they do help federal immigration officers by treating them as if they work for the federal government.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

bills.title: Private Student Loan Bankruptcy Fairness Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Private Student Loan Bankruptcy Fairness Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get rid of private student loan debt when they file for bankruptcy. Right now, both government and private student loans are very hard to erase in bankruptcy because you have to prove they cause serious financial hardship. The bill changes the rules so that private student loans can be discharged in bankruptcy just like credit card debt or other regular debts. Government student loans would still keep their special protection and be hard to discharge. This means people with private student loans would have more options to get a fresh start if they cannot pay their debts.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.plain_summary: This law makes the Department of Homeland Security write a report every month about people who try to enter the United States illegally and might be dangerous to our country's safety. The reports must say how many of these people were caught, what countries they came from, and where they were found trying to cross the border. The government will publish these reports so people can see this information. This helps keep track of people who might pose security risks to the United States.

The agency has 180 days to put this rule back in place. Once the rule is remade, no one can take it to court to challenge it. bills.title: Rural Broadband Protection Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the Federal Communications Commission create new rules to check if companies are ready before giving them government money to build internet in rural areas. Companies that want this money must show they have the right skills, enough money, and good plans to actually build the internet networks they promise. The FCC will look at how well these companies did with other government programs in the past. Companies that lie or fail during this checking process will have to pay fines. bills.title: PRINTS Act
bills.plain_summary: The PRINTS Act helps protect children who might be victims of human trafficking at the US border. It requires border agents to fingerprint children under 14 years old if they suspect the child is being trafficked or used by adults who are not their real family members. The bill makes it a crime for adults to use children who are not their close relatives to help them enter the country illegally. Government agencies must share information about these children with each other and report to Congress about how many children are fingerprinted and how many fake families are caught.

Right now, these plans can only last for three months and can be extended to four months total. This new law would let people buy short-term health insurance that lasts for almost a full year and can be renewed for up to three years total. bills.title: Grant’s Law
bills.plain_summary: Grant's Law says that when someone who is in the United States illegally gets arrested for certain crimes, the government must keep them in jail even before they are found guilty. The Department of Homeland Security has to hold these people until their immigration case is finished, which must happen within 90 days. If the person is let out for their criminal court case, the government has to take them back into custody right after. Even if the person is found not guilty of the crime they were arrested for, they still have to stay in jail until their immigration case is done.

bills.short_title: Preserving Safe Communities by Ending Swatting Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it a crime to lie to emergency services like police or fire departments on purpose. This fake reporting is called swatting and it wastes emergency resources and can hurt innocent people. If someone gets hurt or killed because of the false report, the person who lied will face more serious punishment. People who are harmed by swatting can also sue the person who made the false report.

bills.plain_summary: The FAIR PREP Act of 2025 stops the IRS from preparing tax returns for people, including through its Direct File program that lets people file taxes for free on the government's website. The bill says the IRS cannot fill out tax forms or file tax returns for taxpayers. However, the IRS can still help people through programs where they work with private companies or community groups to offer free tax help. The Treasury Department also cannot spend money to create new tax preparation services without permission from Congress.

bills.plain_summary: This bill would get rid of a law from 1993 that makes it easier for people to register to vote. If this bill passes, states would not have to offer those convenient ways to register anymore. People would still be able to vote, but they might have to work harder to sign up first.

bills.title: America First Act
bills.plain_summary: The America First Act would cut off many government benefits for immigrants who are not full citizens or permanent residents. This includes people seeking asylum, those with temporary protection, and DREAMERS who would lose access to food stamps, Medicaid, housing help, and tax credits. The bill also cuts school funding in half for cities that do not help with immigration enforcement and stops charities from using federal money to help certain immigrants. The law would make the child tax credit permanent for eligible families but would require people to prove their immigration status before getting most government benefits.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for what food people can buy with SNAP benefits, which are like food stamps that help families buy groceries. People will no longer be able to use SNAP to buy soft drinks, candy, ice cream, or prepared desserts like cookies and cakes. bills.title: Proposing a Federal debt limit amendment to the Constitution of the United States.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to add a new rule to the Constitution that would limit how much money the federal government can borrow. The government could only owe up to 130 percent of all the money the country makes in a year, and this limit would slowly go down to 120 percent over time. The only exception would be if the country is at war or facing a serious threat to national security, when the government could spend more money on the military. The President would have to make sure that all government budgets follow these new borrowing limits.

bills.short_title: Health Care Efficiency Through Flexibility Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives healthcare groups called accountable care organizations more time and flexibility in how they report quality information to the government. Instead of having to use a new electronic reporting system right away, these organizations can wait until 2030 to switch. The bill also creates a test program to try out different digital ways of reporting and requires the government to make better standards that work for all kinds of healthcare groups. Until the new standards are ready in 2030, these organizations can keep using their current reporting methods.

bills.title: Red Light Act
bills.plain_summary: The Red Light Act would stop the federal government from giving highway money to states that give driver's licenses or ID cards to people who are in the country illegally. This includes money used to fix roads, make roads safer, and reduce traffic pollution. If a state has laws that let people without legal permission to be in the US get these documents, they would lose this federal funding starting in 2023. States could get their highway money back if they change their laws to stop giving licenses and ID cards to people here illegally.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

bills.title: Noncontiguous Shipping Relief Act of 2024
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes shipping rules to allow foreign ships to carry goods between the mainland United States and places like Hawaii, Alaska, and Puerto Rico. Right now, only American-built ships with mostly American crews can do this kind of shipping. The foreign ships would still have to follow American safety and environmental rules and meet worker protection standards. They would also have to hire American crew members and follow other US laws. This could help reduce shipping costs to these areas that are not connected to the mainland by land.
bills.last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR E90-91)

bills.title: Protecting Higher Education from Foreign Threats Act
bills.short_title: Protecting Higher Education from Foreign Threats Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops colleges and universities from getting federal money if they have teachers who are paid by the Chinese Communist Party while teaching students. If a school has teachers getting money from the Chinese Communist Party, the school will lose all federal funding. Schools can get their federal money back if they prove they no longer employ these teachers. The new rule starts 180 days after the law is signed.

bills.title: Restoring Confidence in the World Anti-Doping Agency Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Restoring Confidence in the World Anti-Doping Agency Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes two main changes to how the United States handles Olympic sports and drug testing. First, it officially changes the name of the US Olympic Committee to include Paralympic athletes, calling it the US Olympic and Paralympic Committee. Second, it gives a government office new power over the World Anti-Doping Agency, which is the group that makes sure athletes don't cheat by using drugs. The government office must make sure the United States and individual athletes have fair say in how this anti-doping group works. If the anti-doping group doesn't treat the US fairly, America can stop paying its membership fees.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of 2025 creates a huge tax on big oil and gas companies that pollute the air. The government will collect one trillion dollars from these companies based on how much harmful gas they put into the air between 2000 and 2023. All this money will go into a special fund to help when bad weather disasters happen, make communities stronger against storms and floods, and help people who are hurt most by climate change. The law still lets people sue these companies in court and lets states and cities make their own climate rules too.

bills.title: To amend title 38, United States Code, to establish qualifications for the appointment of a person as a marriage and family therapist, qualified to provide clinical supervision, in the Veterans Health Administration.
bills.plain_summary: This bill sets new rules for marriage and family therapists who want to supervise other therapists at Veterans Affairs hospitals. To get this job, they must have a master's degree and be licensed in their state. They also must be allowed by their state to supervise other therapists, or be approved as a supervisor by a national marriage and family therapy group. These new requirements will help make sure veterans get good care from well-trained therapists.

bills.title: Stay on Schedule (S.O.S.) Resolution
bills.plain_summary: This resolution would change the rules for the House of Representatives to make sure they finish their work on time. Currently, Congress takes a break in August even if they haven't finished passing the spending bills that fund the government for the next year. Under this new rule, the House would not be allowed to go on their August break until they pass all the required spending bills by July 31st. If the bills aren't done, House members would have to stay and work through August until all the spending legislation is completed.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

bills.title: Restoring Trade Fairness Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill ends the special trade deal the United States has with China and puts big taxes called tariffs on goods coming from China. Most Chinese products will have taxes of at least 35%, while some high-tech items and important materials will have taxes of 100%. The higher taxes will be added slowly over five years, and the money collected will go into a special fund to help American businesses that get hurt when China fights back. The bill also stops small packages from China, North Korea, Russia, and Iran from getting free shipping into the US without paying taxes.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Coast Guard and Maritime Transportation.

bills.title: WISER Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The WISER Act of 2025 helps women veterans who were forced to leave military service between 1951 and 1976 because they became pregnant, had a baby, or became a parent through adoption or custody. These women were kicked out of the military under an old rule called Executive Order 10240. The bill creates two programs to help them: one upgrades their military discharge records to make them better, and another gives them a one-time payment of twenty-five thousand dollars. Women veterans must apply for these programs, and if a veteran has died, her surviving spouse can still apply for the money.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.

bills.title: SWAMP Act
bills.plain_summary: The SWAMP Act would force most government agency headquarters to move out of the Washington DC area. States and local governments could compete to have these agencies move to their areas instead. Agencies that are already in DC cannot build new buildings or sign new leases for their offices. The government would pay for these moves by using money it already has and by selling buildings.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for how much people can subtract from their federal taxes when they pay state and local taxes. bills.plain_summary: This bill changes special savings accounts that were only for school into accounts that can be used for learning new job skills throughout your whole life. People can put money into these accounts until they turn 70 years old and get tax breaks for doing so. Companies can also put money into their workers' accounts and get tax benefits too. bills.title: Protecting Military Parental Leave Evaluations Act
bills.short_title: Protecting Military Parental Leave Evaluations Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Defense to make new rules about parental leave for people in the military. The rules will protect military parents from getting bad performance reviews if they take more than 31 days of parental leave in a row. The bill also makes it easier for military parents to use their leave time within two years of having a baby, adopting a child, or getting custody of a child without needing special permission. These changes will help military families take care of their children without hurting their careers.

bills.title: Illegitimate Court Counteraction Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill punishes the International Criminal Court when it tries to investigate or arrest Americans or people from US ally countries that never agreed to let the court have power over them. If the court goes after these protected people, the President must block certain foreign individuals from entering the US and freeze their money and property. The bill also stops all US government money from going to the International Criminal Court. The punishments also apply to family members of people who help the court with these cases.

bills.title: Restricting First Use of Nuclear Weapons Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Restricting First Use of Nuclear Weapons Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the President cannot use nuclear weapons to attack first unless Congress declares war. A first attack means using nuclear weapons before any other country has used nuclear weapons against the United States or its allies. The bill states that using nuclear weapons first without Congress saying yes would break the Constitution. It also stops the government from spending money on first nuclear attacks unless Congress agrees to war.

bills.title: Proxy Voting for New Parents Resolution
bills.short_title: Proxy Voting for New Parents Resolution
bills.plain_summary: This resolution lets House members who just had a baby or whose spouse had a baby vote through another member for up to 12 weeks. It also applies to pregnant members who cannot travel safely or have serious medical problems. To use proxy voting, members must write a signed letter saying why they need it and which other member will vote for them. The proxy votes count in regular voting but not when counting how many members are present for a quorum. Members can cancel their proxy voting by showing up and voting themselves or by writing another letter.

bills.title: Spectrum Pipeline Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This law allows the government to sell radio wave licenses to private companies again through auctions, which they haven't been able to do since 2023. It also requires the government to find at least 2,500 megahertz of radio waves that government agencies currently use and give them to private companies or make them free for everyone to use. At least half of these radio waves must be sold to companies like cell phone providers, while another portion will be set aside for things like Wi-Fi that anyone can use for free. The law gives the government eight years to complete this process and requires regular reports to Congress on their progress. When government agencies have to move to new radio frequencies, they can now get money faster to upgrade their equipment.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about who pays first when Medicaid patients also have other health insurance. Right now, Medicaid sometimes pays first for children's checkups and then tries to get the money back from other insurance companies later. The new law would make other insurance companies always pay first before Medicaid pays anything. bills.title: Defense of Conscience in Health Care Act
bills.short_title: Defense of Conscience in Health Care Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Health and Human Services to create new rules that protect healthcare workers who don't want to do certain medical procedures because of their religious or personal beliefs. The new rules must be exactly like older rules from 2019 that were supposed to protect these workers but were thrown out by courts before they could be used. The 2019 rules had stronger punishments for hospitals or clinics that forced workers to do things against their beliefs. Healthcare workers would be protected if they refused to do things like provide abortions or refer patients for abortions. The government has six months to put these protective rules back in place.

bills.title: TAKE IT DOWN Act

Online websites and apps must create a way for victims to report these images and remove them within 48 hours. People who break this law can face jail time, fines, and must pay money to help the victims.

bills.title: Freedom from Mandates Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill cancels two rules made by the President that required COVID-19 vaccines for federal workers and people who work for companies that do business with the government. It also stops government agencies from making new rules that would force employers to require their workers to get COVID-19 vaccines or get tested if they are not vaccinated. The bill specifically prevents the Department of Labor from requiring workplace vaccine mandates and stops the Department of Health and Human Services from requiring healthcare providers to mandate vaccines for their employees. If passed, this law would remove existing COVID-19 vaccine requirements and prevent new ones from being created.

bills.plain_summary: The No Support for Terror Act stops the United States from giving money to countries that commit genocide or support terrorism. The bill tells the Treasury Department to oppose giving special international money called Special Drawing Rights to these bad countries through the International Monetary Fund. Any organization that gets this aid money must prove they are following U.S. laws that prevent money from going to terrorists.

bills.title: FairTax Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The FairTax Act of 2025 would get rid of all federal income taxes, payroll taxes, and estate taxes starting in 2027. Instead, the government would collect money through a 23% national sales tax on things people buy. Families at or below the poverty level would get monthly rebate checks to help pay for the extra sales tax costs. States would collect this federal sales tax along with their own state taxes. The bill would also shut down the IRS after 2029 and create new tax agencies, but the whole plan would end if the constitutional amendment allowing income taxes is not repealed within seven years.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: DRAIN THE SWAMP Act
bills.plain_summary: This law forces government agencies to move at least 30 percent of their Washington DC workers to offices in other states within one year. Agencies must also get rid of at least 30 percent of their office space in Washington DC within two years. Workers who get moved cannot work from home full time anymore, and they will be paid based on their new location. Some important agencies like the Department of Defense and CIA do not have to follow these rules. The goal is to spread government jobs around the country instead of keeping them all in Washington DC.

bills.title: Early Participation in Regulations Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Early Participation in Regulations Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires government agencies to give early warning before making major new rules that could cost the economy at least 100 million dollars per year. Agencies must publish an advance notice at least 90 days before proposing the actual rule. The advance notice must explain what problem the rule is trying to fix, what other options they are considering, and give people at least 30 days to send in their comments. The goal is to let the public know earlier about important rules so they can participate in the process before decisions are made.

bills.title: Pony Up Act
bills.plain_summary: The Pony Up Act makes the Postal Service pay people back when they get charged late fees because their mail was delivered too slowly. If the Postal Service gets a bill at least 12 days before it is due but delivers it less than 6 days before the due date, or if they get a payment 5 days early but deliver it late, they have to pay any late fees the person got charged. The Postal Service does not have to pay if the delay was caused by something they could not control, like a natural disaster. The bill also makes the Postal Service report to Congress every year about mail delays and requires them to study whether big companies get better mail service than regular people.

bills.title: .Proposing a balanced budget amendment to the Constitution requiring that each agency and department's funding is justified.
bills.plain_summary: This constitutional amendment would require the federal government to spend no more money than it takes in each year, creating a balanced budget. The government could only spend more than it earns if three out of five members in both the House and Senate vote to allow it, and total spending cannot be more than a certain percentage of what the entire country produces. Any tax increases or borrowing more money would also need approval from three out of five members of Congress. The President would have to create a budget that does not spend more than the government earns and explain why each government agency needs the money it is asking for. The rules could be ignored only during wars, military threats, or natural disasters.

bills.plain_summary: This bill says that before government agencies can make any new rule, they must first get rid of three old rules. If the new rule would cost a lot of money, it cannot cost more than the three old rules that were removed. Agencies also have to tell Congress within 90 days which of their current rules cost too much money or do not work well. The goal is to reduce the total number of government rules by making agencies remove more rules than they add.

bills.title: Ending Presidential Overreach on Public Lands Act
bills.short_title: Ending Presidential Overreach on Public Lands Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill takes away the President's power to create new national monuments or make existing national monuments bigger. Instead, only Congress would be allowed to create new national monuments or expand the ones that already exist. Right now, the President can set aside land to protect it as a national monument without asking Congress first. If this bill becomes law, the President would no longer be able to do that on their own.

bills.title: Student Empowerment Act
bills.plain_summary: The Student Empowerment Act changes the rules for 529 education savings accounts so families can use the money for kindergarten through high school expenses, not just college costs. Parents can now use these tax-free savings to pay for things like school books, tutoring, test fees, and special help for students with disabilities. The money can be used whether kids go to public schools, private schools, religious schools, or are taught at home. This gives families more choices about how to spend their education savings to help their children learn.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes changes to the North Pacific Research Board, which gives money to scientists who study fish and ocean life around Alaska. The bill adds one more member to the board who must be an Alaska Native with experience using natural resources for food and materials. It also makes it easier for the board to spend money on running their office, especially when they get less funding than the year before. bills.title: Midnight Rules Relief Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about how Congress can reject regulations made by the President. Right now, Congress can only reject one regulation at a time during the President's last year in office. This new bill would let Congress group together many regulations from the President's final year and reject them all with just one vote. This would make it faster and easier for Congress to overturn multiple rules that were made near the end of a President's time in office.

bills.title: Railroad Responsibility Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill lets states make their own rules about how long trains can block road crossings. Right now, federal law stops states from making these rules, so when trains block roads for a long time, states cannot do much about it. The bill changes this by saying federal transportation laws will not override state laws that limit how long railroad companies can block crossings. This means states can pass laws to prevent trains from blocking roads and driveways for too long.

bills.title: Endangered Species Transparency and Reasonableness Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Endangered Species Transparency and Reasonableness Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes changes to how the government decides which animals and plants are endangered or threatened. The government must put all the scientific information they use to make these decisions on the internet for everyone to see, except for some private state information and military secrets. Any information that comes from state, tribal, or county governments automatically counts as good scientific data. The government must also keep track of and report how much money they spend on court cases about endangered species laws. Finally, the bill changes the rules about how much money lawyers can get paid when they win these court cases.

If this bill passes, the federal government would no longer have this specific law to prosecute people who interfere with others seeking these services. The repeal would also apply to any court cases that are currently happening or might happen in the future under this law.

bills.plain_summary: This bill gets rid of a government program that made oil and gas companies pay fees when they released too much methane gas into the air. The program was run by the Environmental Protection Agency and was meant to help companies reduce pollution. The bill also takes back any leftover money that was saved for this program. Only big companies that released a lot of greenhouse gases had to pay these fees.

bills.title: United States-Cuba Trade Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill would end the trade embargo between the United States and Cuba that has been in place for many years. It would cancel laws that stopped Americans from doing business with Cuba or traveling there freely. The bill would allow normal trade between the two countries, let Americans visit Cuba without restrictions, and permit phone and internet services between the nations. It would also allow people to send money back and forth between the United States and Cuba.

bills.title: Thin Blue Line Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about when someone can get the death penalty. If someone kills a police officer, firefighter, or other first responder while they are working, the person who killed them could face the death penalty. The bill also applies if someone targets these workers because of their job or because they work for the government. This adds a new reason to the list of crimes that can result in the death penalty.

bills.plain_summary: The MAIN Event Ticketing Act makes new rules to stop people from using computer programs called bots to unfairly buy lots of event tickets online. The law requires ticket websites to have better security systems to protect against these bots and hackers. If someone breaks these rules by using bots or if the website gets attacked, the ticket company must report it to the government within thirty days. The government can fine people who break these rules and will work with police to catch ticket bot users.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the Endangered Species Act to make the government treat wild animals and animals born in captivity exactly the same. Right now, the law sometimes treats these two groups differently when making decisions about protecting endangered species. Under this new rule, it would not matter if an animal was born in the wild or raised by people when the government decides how to protect that species. bills.title: Deport Alien Gang Members Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates new rules to keep people connected to criminal gangs out of the United States and to remove them if they are already here. It defines criminal gangs as groups of five or more people whose main goal is committing serious crimes. If someone is a gang member or has helped a gang with illegal activities, they cannot enter the country and must be deported if they are found here. These people also cannot get asylum, special protection, or other immigration benefits, and they must be kept in detention. The Department of Homeland Security can officially label groups as criminal gangs using a special process.

bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new government grant program to help save sea turtles that are sick, hurt, or trapped. The bill also sets up a special emergency fund that the government can use quickly when sea turtles need urgent help. This new program works just like an existing program that already helps rescue marine mammals like whales and dolphins.

bills.short_title: Recruiting Families Using Data Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires states to make detailed plans to find and keep more foster and adoptive families. The plans must include input from families and young people who have experience with foster care. States will need to work on keeping foster children in stable homes, placing more kids with relatives, and finding diverse families that match the backgrounds of children who need homes. States must also report to Congress each year about how many foster families they have, what problems families face, and how they are working to recruit different types of families.

bills.plain_summary: The AMERICANS Act stops the Defense Secretary from making new COVID-19 vaccine rules for military members. It helps service members who were kicked out or punished for not getting the COVID-19 vaccine by letting them return to service with clean records and get back pay. bills.title: RACE Act
bills.plain_summary: The RACE Act is a new law that sets time limits for counting votes in federal elections like those for President and Congress. States must finish counting all ballots by 10 PM on election day and announce the final results within 48 hours after the election ends. If there is an emergency like a natural disaster or if voting machines break down, states can take longer to count the votes. The goal is to make sure election results come out quickly and people can trust the voting process.

bills.title: Stopping Overdoses of Fentanyl Analogues Act
bills.short_title: Stopping Overdoses of Fentanyl Analogues Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes all fentanyl-related drugs permanently illegal under federal law. Fentanyl-related drugs are chemicals that are similar to fentanyl, which is a very dangerous drug that causes many overdoses. Right now, these drugs are only temporarily banned until March 2025, but this bill would make the ban permanent. The bill puts these drugs in Schedule I, which means they have a high risk of abuse and no accepted medical use, making them completely illegal to make, sell, or use.

bills.title: The HCT/P Modernization Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: The HCT/P Modernization Act of 2025 requires the FDA to make it easier for people to understand the rules about human cells and tissues used in medical treatments. The FDA must hold workshops to teach people about these products and create a way for the public to share their thoughts about the rules. The agency also has to put helpful information on its website and publish details each year about how many companies are registered and how many inspections they do. By September 2026, the FDA must write a report for Congress with ideas on how to make the rules better.

bills.short_title: Defund Heroin Injection Centers Act of 2025
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from giving money to any state, local, tribal, or private group that runs supervised drug injection sites. bills.title: Goldie’s Act
bills.plain_summary: Goldie's Act makes the rules for protecting animals stronger. The government must check on places that keep animals like zoos and research labs at least once a year. If they find animals being hurt or treated badly, they have to take those animals away right away. When someone breaks the animal protection rules, they can be fined up to ten thousand dollars for each thing they did wrong. The government also has to quickly tell local police and animal control officers when they find someone breaking these rules.

bills.title: Proposing a balanced budget amendment to the Constitution of the United States.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to add a new rule to the Constitution that says the government cannot spend more money than it takes in each year. If Congress wants to spend more money than the government receives, two-thirds of both the House and Senate would have to vote yes to allow it. The President would also have to propose budgets that don't spend more than the government gets. This new rule would not count money used to pay back loans the government already owes. The rule would start working five years after enough states agree to make it part of the Constitution.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Historic Roadways Protection Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the Bureau of Land Management from spending money to close old historic roads on public land in Utah. Utah and 22 counties are fighting in court to keep these old roads open because they say they have legal rights to use them from an old 1866 law. The bill says the government cannot finish or start new plans to manage travel in certain Utah areas until the courts decide who is right about these road rights. It also stops four existing travel plans from being put into action until the court cases are over.

bills.plain_summary: This bill creates rules to cut funding for government programs that don't have proper approval from Congress. If a program loses its authorization, it will have its funding cut by 10 percent in the first year, then 15 percent in the second and third years. After three years without authorization, the program will be completely shut down unless Congress votes to approve it again. The goal is to make sure all government programs have current legal permission to spend taxpayer money.
bills.last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 25 - 19.
bills.subjects_str: Appropriations, Budget process, Executive agency funding and structure, Government information and archives, Legislative rules and procedure

bills.title: No Hires for the Delinquent IRS Act
bills.short_title: No Hires for the Delinquent IRS Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the IRS from hiring any new workers until they can prove none of their current employees owe seriously overdue taxes. Seriously overdue taxes means the government has already put a legal claim on someone's property because they haven't paid. However, it doesn't count people who are already making payment plans, asked for a hearing, or have other special situations. The Treasury Secretary must write an official letter saying all IRS workers have paid their taxes before the agency can hire anyone new.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_keywords_str: IRS hiring freeze, IRS employees owe taxes, government workers tax debt, IRS staff unpaid taxes, Treasury Secretary certification, federal employees tax liens, IRS hiring ban, government agency hiring stop, tax collectors owing taxes, IRS worker tax compliance, federal hiring restrictions, delinquent taxes IRS staff

bills.title: Inaction Has Consequences Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill says that members of Congress will not get their paychecks if their part of Congress does not pass all the yearly spending bills on time. The House and Senate each have to pass these bills before October 1st, which is when the government's budget year starts. If they don't finish the work on time, their pay gets put aside and they can't have it until they pass all the bills or until their time in Congress ends. This rule would start in 2026 and is meant to make Congress members work harder to avoid government shutdowns.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

bills.title: Protecting our Communities from Sexual Predators Act
bills.short_title: Protecting our Communities from Sexual Predators Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules about non-citizens who commit sexual assault crimes in America. If a non-citizen is arrested or charged with sexual assault, they must be held in jail and cannot be released. Non-citizens who are convicted of sexual assault cannot enter the United States and must be removed from the country if they are already here. The bill is meant to protect communities by making sure sexual predators who are not American citizens are kept away from the public.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to require that the Supreme Court of the United States be composed of nine justices.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to change the Constitution to make sure the Supreme Court always has exactly nine judges. Right now, the Constitution doesn't say how many judges the Supreme Court must have, so Congress could change that number if they wanted to. The bill says there should be one Chief Justice and eight other justices, and that number could never be changed. To make this part of the Constitution, two thirds of Congress and three fourths of all the states would have to agree to it.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Tennessee Valley Authority Salary Transparency Act
bills.short_title: Tennessee Valley Authority Salary Transparency Act
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the Tennessee Valley Authority report more information to Congress each year. The Tennessee Valley Authority is a government company that provides electricity to Tennessee and nearby states. Starting again, they must file a yearly financial report with Congress every March. The report must include the names, salaries, and job duties of management workers who earn more than a certain government pay level, but this salary information stays private and is not shared with the public.
bills.last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.
